proposals.title: A Unified, Phenotype-Enabled Search and Analysis Platform for Public Sequence Archives
proposals.motivation: Public sequence archives like the Sequence Read Archive (SRA) contain petabytes of data from diverse organisms, including human, coral, and viral pathogens like ZIKV, but remain notoriously difficult to search beyond basic metadata. This limits the reuse of data for novel analyses, such as identifying healthy versus diseased (e.g., SCTLD) coral transcriptomes or finding genomes with specific phenotypic traits. The lack of integrated, computable phenotype annotations severely hinders data discovery and secondary analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by programmatically extracting and standardizing phenotype and experimental condition annotations from published literature and metadata, and linking them to sequence data, we can build a search engine that enables biologists to discover datasets based on biological outcomes and sample characteristics, not just sequencing parameters.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that uses named entity recognition (NER) with models like SciBERT to extract phenotypic terms (e.g., 'bleaching', 'resistance') from publications linked to SRA BioProjects. These terms will be mapped to ontologies (e.g., HPO, ENVO). For viral and bacterial data, we will integrate pre-2026 tools like Kraken2 and ABRicate to annotate sequences with antimicrobial resistance or virulence markers. The search backend will use an Elasticsearch index combining this enriched annotation with standardized metadata and pre-computed k-mer sketches (via Mash) for fast sequence-similarity searches.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source, web-accessible platform that transforms how researchers query public sequence data, enabling discovery by phenotype and genotype. It will directly support research in areas central to Salzberg's lab, such as comparative genomics of healthy/diseased corals and pathogen surveillance. The platform will serve as a benchmark for integrating heterogeneous biological data types.
proposals.novelty: While tools exist for searching sequences by similarity or metadata, a system that programmatically mines and links rich, computable phenotype data from the literature to specific archived sequencing runs at scale is a novel integration of natural language processing, ontology management, and genomic analysis pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Secure, Portable, and Scalable Architecture for Personal Genomic Data Vaults Using DNA-Based Micro-Archives
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of personal whole-genome sequencing creates immense data control and long-term storage challenges. Centralized databases pose privacy risks and accessibility issues, while existing digital storage is fragile over decades. A decentralized, physically secure, and user-controlled solution is urgently needed to empower individuals with true ownership of their genomic data, a critical resource for future personalized medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an integrated system combining highly compressed genomic data formats with emerging DNA-based storage can create a durable, portable, and secure 'data vault' that places complete control of genomic information in the hands of the individual.
proposals.method: We will design a micro-archive architecture leveraging the T2T-CHM13 human reference genome for coordinate-based compression of diploid variant calls (SNPs, indels, SVs). The compressed data will be encoded into DNA sequences using robust error-correcting codes (e.g., fountain codes) optimized for synthesis and sequencing by synthesis (Illumina) platforms. A prototype will include a secure, open-source software suite for local encryption, encoding, and decoding, paired with a physical storage medium (e.g., silica-encapsulated DNA) designed for ambient stability. We will benchmark against standard FASTQ/BAM file sizes and simulate long-term data recovery.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first practical blueprint for a personal genomic data vault, merging bioinformatics compression with molecular storage. It will deliver a prototype software toolkit and a rigorous analysis of the feasibility, cost, and error tolerance of DNA as a long-term, individual-centric genomic archive, shifting the paradigm from cloud-based to physical data custody.
proposals.novelty: While DNA data storage is being explored for large archives, its application to personal, portable genomic vaults with integrated compression and encryption for individual control is novel. This approach directly addresses the unique requirements of genomic data—privacy, permanence, and portability—in a single system.

proposals.title: A Scalable, Real-Time Genomic Surveillance Platform for Global Pathogen Monitoring
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the critical need for rapid, global pathogen genomic surveillance to track variants and inform public health responses. However, existing systems are fragmented, rely on manual data submission and curation, and lack real-time analytical pipelines, creating dangerous delays. A unified, automated system is urgently needed to integrate and analyze sequencing data from diverse pathogens, from HIV and Zika virus (ZIKV) to emerging threats, as they are generated worldwide.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing a cloud-based, automated data ingestion and analysis framework, we can establish a real-time global pathogen surveillance system that dramatically reduces the time from sequence generation to actionable variant report, enabling faster public health interventions.
proposals.method: We will build a cloud-native platform using a microservices architecture for scalable data ingestion from public repositories (NCBI SRA, GISAID) and direct lab submissions. Core analysis will leverage and extend our prior methods for rapid, reference-guided assembly and variant calling (as used for HIV-1 and human diploid genomes). We will implement a modular, pathogen-agnostic analysis pipeline using Nextflow for workflow management, integrating tools for phylogenetic placement (UShER), lineage assignment (Pangolin), and functional annotation of protein changes to assess phenotypic impact, inspired by our phenotype-to-genotype work.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, end-to-end platform for real-time genomic surveillance of multiple pathogens, providing a public good for global health. It will establish new benchmarks for data integration speed and analytical throughput, directly applicable to ongoing threats like SCTLD in corals and arboviruses like ZIKV. The system will empower researchers and health agencies with immediate, standardized genomic intelligence.
proposals.novelty: While individual surveillance tools exist, this proposal integrates them into a fully automated, scalable production system designed for continuous operation, moving beyond batch processing. Its pathogen-agnostic design, driven by lessons from diverse projects (human, viral, coral), and focus on real-time protein-level annotation for threat assessment, represents a significant advance over current siloed efforts.

proposals.title: An Integrated, Shotgun-First Sequencing and Mapping Strategy for Rapid, High-Quality De Novo Assembly of Complex Genomes
proposals.motivation: De novo genome assembly for complex, non-model organisms like corals or novel pathogens remains challenging, often requiring multiple rounds of sequencing and costly, time-consuming hierarchical strategies. The Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) crisis and the need for rapid genomic characterization of emerging viruses like ZIKV highlight the urgent need for more efficient, single-pass assembly pipelines that produce high-quality, chromosome-scale references. Current methods often treat long-range scaffolding as a separate, secondary phase, creating bottlenecks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that initiating a project with a fully integrated strategy, combining high-coverage, short-read shotgun sequencing with simultaneous long-range optical or Hi-C mapping from the same DNA extraction, will yield more contiguous, accurate, and complete de novo assemblies in a single workflow than traditional sequential approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop and benchmark a unified bioinformatics pipeline. DNA from target samples (e.g., SCTLD-affected corals, ZIKV isolates) will be processed in parallel for Illumina shotgun sequencing and Bionano Genomics optical mapping or Dovetail Omni-C. Data will be integrated from the start using a modified assembler architecture, where contigs from a SPAdes or MaSuRCA assembly are directly scaffolded with mapping data via the SALSA2 or Bionano Solve algorithm, iteratively refining the assembly graph. The resulting assemblies will be validated against known benchmarks (e.g., human diploid genome) and metrics like N50, BUSCO completeness, and structural variant accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, optimized protocol for rapid de novo genome construction, directly applicable to conservation genomics (coral disease) and pathogen surveillance. It will deliver high-quality reference genomes for critical non-model species and demonstrate a paradigm shift from sequential to parallelized genomic data acquisition and analysis.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid assembly is established, the novelty lies in formally testing and optimizing a 'shotgun-first, map-simultaneously' primary strategy as a unified standard, rather than treating long-range mapping as an optional add-on, and rigorously quantifying its benefits for speed and assembly quality in applied, pre-2026 contexts like disease ecology.

proposals.title: De novo Gene Annotation from Short Reads: A Probabilistic Framework for Inferring Gene Models Without Genome Assembly
proposals.motivation: Current gene annotation pipelines are critically dependent on high-quality, contiguous genome assemblies, which are expensive and computationally intensive to produce, especially for non-model organisms, metagenomic samples, or complex diploid genomes. This bottleneck limits rapid genomic analysis in critical areas like emerging pathogen surveillance (e.g., ZIKV), coral disease studies (e.g., SCTLD), and large-scale population genomics. A method to directly annotate genes from raw sequencing data would dramatically accelerate discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model, trained on the statistical relationships between short-read sequence features and complete gene annotations from model organisms, can directly and accurately predict gene models—including exon-intron boundaries and alternative isoforms—from short-read data alone, bypassing the need for de novo genome assembly.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep learning framework, likely a hybrid graph neural network (GNN) and transformer architecture. The model will ingest multiple data streams from short-read libraries: k-mer spectra, read-pair linkage graphs, and integrated signals from matched RNA-seq and ATAC-seq (where available). It will be trained on extensive, high-quality annotations from the human genome (e.g., CHM13) and other model organisms. The output will be a probabilistic annotation of gene features directly onto the cloud of sequencing reads, using a CRF layer for structured prediction of gene models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel software tool that enables rapid, cost-effective gene discovery in samples where genome assembly is impractical or impossible. It will provide a new paradigm for annotating complex genomic regions, metagenomes, and highly polymorphic populations, directly supporting research in virology, marine biology, and personalized genomics.
proposals.novelty: While existing tools like BRAKER1 use extrinsic evidence for annotation, they require an assembled genome. Our approach is fundamentally novel by eliminating the assembly scaffold entirely, instead treating gene annotation as a direct inference problem on fragmented data, analogous to archaeological reconstruction.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Assembly-Independent Annotation Pipeline for Pathogen and Environmental Genomics Using Short-Read Sequencing
proposals.motivation: Accurate gene annotation is foundational for functional genomics, yet many studies of emerging pathogens (e.g., ZIKV variants) and non-model organisms (e.g., corals affected by SCTLD) rely on short-read sequencing, which produces fragmented assemblies. Current annotation tools like BRAKER2 and AUGUSTUS depend heavily on contiguous reference genomes or long scaffolds, creating a critical bottleneck for analyzing diverse, fragmented datasets where *de novo* assembly yields only short contigs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel computational pipeline, which bypasses traditional genome assembly, can directly annotate genes from short-read sequencing data by integrating deep learning-based *ab initio* prediction with protein homology and transcriptome evidence, achieving accuracy comparable to scaffold-based methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that processes raw or error-corrected short reads. First, it will use a transformer-based neural network (inspired by DNABERT but trained for gene boundary detection) to predict potential coding regions directly from k-mer spectra of read clusters. These predictions will be integrated with evidence from: 1) alignment of reads to a curated protein database (using DIAMOND in a fast, assembly-free mode), and 2) *de novo* transcriptome assemblies from the same samples. A consensus annotation will be generated using a graph-based reconciliation algorithm, evaluated against gold-standard annotations from completed genomes (e.g., the human diploid benchmark) and simulated fragmented datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, assembly-independent annotation tool specifically designed for fragmented genomic data, directly applicable to pathogen surveillance (e.g., HIV-1, ZIKV) and environmental metagenomics. It will expand the utility of legacy and cost-effective short-read data, enabling more reliable genotype-to-phenotype studies in non-model systems where reference genomes are absent or poorly assembled.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods require an assembled genome as input, our approach is novel in performing annotation directly on sequence reads or very short contigs, effectively decoupling gene finding from the assembly step. This integrates deep learning for direct read analysis in a way not yet standardized in pre-2026 annotation pipelines.

proposals.title: Mapping Host Genetic Determinants of the Root Microbiome in Populus for Marker-Assisted Breeding of Symbiotic Traits
proposals.motivation: Forest health and productivity are increasingly threatened by climate change and pathogens. The plant microbiome is a critical, heritable component of plant fitness, influencing nutrient acquisition and stress resilience. However, pre-2026 breeding programs focus almost exclusively on direct host traits, neglecting the genetic basis of beneficial host-microbiome interactions as a selectable characteristic.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific host genetic loci (QTLs/SNPs) in forest trees are significantly associated with the abundance and composition of root-associated microbial consortia known to confer beneficial phenotypes, such as growth promotion and disease suppression.
proposals.method: We will establish a large cohort of genetically characterized *Populus* genotypes from breeding programs. Root and rhizosphere samples will undergo deep, shotgun metagenomic sequencing to profile microbial communities, including rare taxa. Host genetic markers (SNPs) will be analyzed against microbial abundance data using mixed linear models (e.g., GEMMA) for microbiome-wide association studies (MWAS), controlling for environmental covariates. Significant host loci will be validated in controlled greenhouse experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution map of host genetic control over the tree microbiome, identifying candidate genes for symbiosis. It will deliver a novel set of molecular markers for breeding programs, enabling the selection of tree genotypes optimized for specific, beneficial microbial partnerships to enhance sustainability.
proposals.novelty: While MWAS are established in humans and crops, applying this framework to perennial trees for deliberate breeding of microbiome traits is a paradigm shift. It moves beyond correlative studies to treat the microbiome as a heritable, engineered component of the host phenotype.

proposals.title: A Complete, Haplotype-Resolved Annotation of Repetitive Element Diversity and Evolution in the Diploid Human Genome
proposals.motivation: Despite the completion of the T2T-CHM13 telomere-to-telomere reference, the functional and evolutionary dynamics of the ~82% repetitive fraction of the human genome remain poorly characterized in diploid individuals. This gap limits our understanding of how repeat variation contributes to phenotypic diversity, disease susceptibility, and genome stability. A comprehensive, haplotype-phased analysis is needed to move beyond a single reference and understand repetitive element biology in a population context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of functionally relevant genetic variation between individuals and between haplotypes resides within complex repetitive regions, and that the evolutionary history of these elements can be reconstructed through comparative analysis of complete, phased diploid assemblies.
proposals.method: We will leverage the recently developed complete diploid human genome benchmark (e.g., HG002) to perform a haplotype-resolved annotation of all repetitive elements using a multi-tool integration pipeline (RepeatModeler2, Dfam, and manual curation). We will then apply comparative genomics methods, adapted from our work on HIV and coral (SCTLD) genomes, to analyze repeat polymorphism, recent activity (L1, Alu), and evolutionary divergence between haplotypes and across primate lineages, using the T2T-CHM13 assembly as an evolutionary anchor.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, haplotype-aware map of repetitive element variation for a complete diploid human genome, serving as a foundational resource for personalized genomics. It will directly link specific repeat variants to annotated genes and regulatory regions, providing a framework to interpret non-coding variation in medical sequencing studies.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have cataloged repeats in a haploid reference, this proposal uniquely focuses on diploid, phased variation and evolution, applying comparative genomic techniques typically used for viral and coral genomes to the complex repetitive landscape of an individual human.

proposals.title: Structural Annotation of NLR Immune Receptors in Pine: Predicting Functional Specificity from Conserved Surface Motifs
proposals.motivation: Pine forests face severe threats from pathogens like the fungus causing white pine blister rust, but the genomic basis of resistance is poorly understood. The large, diverse NLR (Nucleotide-binding domain and Leucine-rich Repeat) gene family is central to plant immunity, yet standard homology-based annotation from crops fails to predict the specific pathogen recognition capabilities of pine NLRs. A method to functionally classify these receptors is critical for identifying resistance genes and informing breeding strategies for forest health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conserved structural motifs and surface residue patterns in pine NLR proteins, particularly within the leucine-rich repeat (LRR) domains, are predictive of their functional specificity and can be used to classify them into putative pathogen-recognition classes.
proposals.method: We will generate high-confidence structural models for pine NLRs using AlphaFold2 and template-based modeling with MODELLER. For each model, we will use the ConSurf server to calculate evolutionary conservation scores and identify conserved surface patches. We will integrate this with motif analysis using MEME/MAST to detect known and novel NLR functional motifs. Machine learning (e.g., random forest or gradient boosting) will be trained on these structural and sequence features, using curated NLR specificity data from Arabidopsis and crops as a preliminary training set, to classify pine NLRs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first structurally informed, functionally annotated catalog of NLR genes in a conifer genome, moving beyond simple gene lists. It will deliver a computational pipeline for NLR functional site prediction that can be applied to other non-model plant species. The resulting specificity predictions will provide direct candidates for experimental validation in pine disease resistance.
proposals.novelty: This adapts established structural bioinformatics tools (ConSurf, AlphaFold2) from general protein function prediction to the specific challenge of annotating a large, complex gene family in a non-model organism, creating a hybrid approach that integrates 3D structural conservation with motif analysis for functional classification.

proposals.title: Metagenomic Binning Strategies for the Deconvolution and Classification of Repetitive Elements in Conifer Genomes
proposals.motivation: Conifer genomes, like that of Pinus taeda, are exceptionally large and complex, dominated by diverse and uncharacterized repetitive sequences that hinder accurate assembly and annotation. Pre-2026 methods for repeat classification struggle with the sheer scale and heterogeneity of these elements. This gap limits our understanding of genome evolution, regulation, and functional genomics in these ecologically and economically vital species.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that algorithms developed for separating microbial genomes from metagenomic mixtures—based on sequence composition and differential coverage—can be repurposed to effectively cluster and classify distinct families of repetitive elements within a single, complex eukaryotic genome.
proposals.method: We will apply established metagenomic binning tools, specifically CONCOCT (which uses sequence composition and coverage) and MetaBAT2, to raw whole-genome shotgun sequencing reads and assembled contigs from Pinus taeda. The 'coverage' signal will be derived from mapping reads from a single individual, treating different repeat families as analogous to distinct 'genomes' in a metagenome based on their k-mer distributions. Resulting bins will be analyzed for repeat content using RepeatMasker and de novo repeat libraries, and benchmarked against traditional repeat classification pipelines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable computational pipeline for dissecting the repeatome of giant genomes. It will generate a more complete and accurate catalog of repetitive elements in pine, serving as a critical resource for future genome assembly and annotation efforts. The successful application will demonstrate a powerful cross-disciplinary method applicable to other complex plant and animal genomes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual cross-pollination: treating a single genome's repetitive landscape as a metagenomic community to be deconvolved, a strategy not previously applied to conifer genomics. This moves beyond homology-based methods to leverage unsupervised clustering algorithms from a different field.

proposals.title: A Complete, Chromosomal-Scale Genome Assembly of the Pine Pathogen Fusarium circinatum Using a Multi-Platform Hybrid Sequencing Strategy
proposals.motivation: Fusarium circinatum, the causal agent of pitch canker, is a devastating fungal pathogen of global pine species, threatening forest ecosystems and timber industries. Pre-2026, genomic studies of this and similar filamentous fungi are hampered by fragmented assemblies due to high repeat content and complex genomic architecture, limiting systems-level analysis of virulence and host-pathogen interactions. A complete, high-quality reference genome is urgently needed to enable functional genomics, population studies, and the development of diagnostic tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid assembly strategy, integrating high-accuracy short-reads, long-reads, and optical mapping data, will overcome the scaffolding challenges posed by repetitive elements and produce a complete, chromosomal-scale genome assembly for Fusarium circinatum. This assembly will be of sufficient quality to serve as a definitive reference for the species.
proposals.method: We will generate Illumina NovaSeq data for high-accuracy base correction, PacBio HiFi or Oxford Nanopore Ultra-Long reads for contiguity, and Bionano Genomics optical maps for long-range scaffolding. Data integration will utilize a custom pipeline building on established tools like Flye or Canu for long-read assembly, Pilon for polishing with short-reads, and Bionano Solve for hybrid scaffolding. We will adapt and extend the assembly reconciliation methods from our prior work on diploid human genomes to fuse these complementary data types into a single, coherent assembly.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first complete, chromosomal-scale reference genome for Fusarium circinatum, a critical resource for the forest pathology community. It will establish a robust hybrid assembly protocol applicable to other challenging fungal genomes. The assembly will directly enable comprehensive studies of pathogenicity genes, comparative genomics across Fusarium species, and the design of targeted molecular assays.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid assembly is established, the systematic, multi-platform integration of HiFi/ONT long-reads with optical mapping specifically for a complex fungal pathogen genome is novel. The project adapts and scales the data fusion principles from large, diploid vertebrate genome projects to a microbial eukaryotic system with distinct genomic challenges, creating a new benchmark for fungal genomics.

proposals.title: De novo transcriptome assembly for non-model corals using a phylogenetically-informed composite reference
proposals.motivation: Many ecologically critical organisms, such as corals affected by Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD), lack high-quality reference genomes due to high heterozygosity, repetitive content, or large size, hindering transcriptomic studies of health and disease. Current methods rely on de novo assembly, which is error-prone for isoforms, or mapping to a single distant relative, losing species-specific information. A robust method to build comprehensive transcriptomes without a genome is urgently needed for conservation genomics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite 'patchwork' reference, constructed from the annotated genomes and transcriptomes of multiple phylogenetically close species, can accurately guide the assembly of a coherent, high-quality transcriptome for a target non-model species using its RNA-seq data alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline that first builds a composite reference by extracting gene models from multiple related species (e.g., Acropora, Orbicella) using pre-2026 tools like GFFread and Cufflinks. RNA-seq reads from the target species will be mapped to this composite using HISAT2 or STAR, with parameters relaxed for cross-species alignment. Conflicting mappings and gaps will be resolved iteratively using StringTie2 for transcript assembly, guided by the read evidence and conserved synteny, followed by PASA for annotation refinement. The final assembly will be benchmarked against de novo (Trinity) and single-reference approaches using BUSCO.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel bioinformatics method and pipeline for transcriptome construction in genome-poor taxa, directly applicable to SCTLD and other coral disease research. It will produce the first well-annotated transcriptome for a target coral species, enabling downstream differential expression and variant analysis. The method generalizes to other non-model systems, advancing the field of ecological and conservation genomics.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit adaptation of a historical linguistics 'reconstruction' analogy to genomics, systematically integrating multiple heterogeneous references rather than one, and using the RNA-seq data itself to iteratively resolve conflicts in a phylogenetically-aware framework, moving beyond simple de novo or single-reference mapping.

proposals.title: DeepSpectralFunc: Probabilistic Functional Element Mapping for Highly Fragmented Genomes Using Spectral Graph Convolutional Networks
proposals.motivation: Many critical organisms, such as corals affected by Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) or novel viral pathogens, have genomes sequenced with short-read technologies, resulting in highly fragmented assemblies. Traditional gene annotation pipelines fail on these assemblies due to their reliance on contiguous open reading frames and homology to complete references. There is a pressing need for methods to extract functional biological insights—coding potential, regulatory elements, non-coding RNAs—directly from the 'shattered' genomic landscape, enabling research on non-model organisms and metagenomic samples.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning model can learn to detect probabilistic functional elements directly from features inherent in fragmented short-read assemblies and their alignments, bypassing the need for contiguous sequence, by integrating local sequence composition, read-pair linkage, and cross-species conservation signals.
proposals.method: We will train a neural network using a spectral graph convolutional architecture, where each scaffold is represented as a graph with nodes (e.g., fixed-length windows) connected by edges weighted by paired-end read co-occurrence and k-mer similarity. Node features will include k-mer frequencies, per-base conservation scores from multi-species short-read alignments (using tools like PhyloP), and per-nucleotide coverage. The model will be trained on fragmented versions of well-annotated genomes (e.g., human, zebrafish) to predict known functional annotations, learning to output per-base probability maps for categories like 'coding', 'regulatory', and 'non-coding RNA'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated tool for probabilistic functional annotation of highly fragmented genomes, directly applicable to coral disease studies, viral discovery (like ZIKV variants), and metagenomic analysis. It will advance the field by shifting the paradigm from deterministic gene calling to statistical functional element mapping, making genomic analysis feasible for thousands of non-model organisms with draft-quality assemblies.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing annotation tools that require contiguity or a close reference, our method is explicitly designed for fragmentation, using a graph neural network to propagate signals across scaffolds based on read-pair links, a novel application of spectral learning in genomics.

proposals.title: A Comprehensive Metagenomic Subtraction Pipeline for the Discovery of Viral and Eukaryotic Pathogens in Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD)
proposals.motivation: Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) is a devastating, pan-Caribbean coral disease whose causative agent(s) remain elusive despite metagenomic studies. Prior analyses, including subtraction approaches focusing on bacterial sequences, have not yielded a definitive pathogen. A comprehensive analysis of the viral and eukaryotic fractions of the SCTLD metagenome is critically needed, as these kingdoms contain known coral pathogens (e.g., viruses in white syndrome, eukaryotic parasites) but are often under-sampled or filtered out in standard pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending a sensitive, multi-kingdom subtraction pipeline to SCTLD metagenomes will identify novel viral or eukaryotic sequences that are significantly enriched in diseased versus healthy coral tissue, providing strong candidates for primary or co-occurring pathogens.
proposals.method: We will extend our established computational subtraction pipeline, which uses efficient k-mer matching (via tools like Kraken2) and alignment (minimap2, BLAST) against comprehensive host reference databases (e.g., *Orbicella faveolata* genome). We will incorporate curated databases for eukaryotic pathogens (NCBI nt, Protist Ribosomal Reference), viral sequences (RefSeq Viral, IMG/VR), and coral-associated microbial eukaryotes. Differential abundance analysis (DESeq2) of assembled contigs and unassembled reads will be performed across healthy and diseased samples to identify statistically significant, non-host signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dedicated catalog of viral and eukaryotic sequences associated with SCTLD, moving the field beyond bacterial candidates. It will deliver a validated, open-source bioinformatics pipeline for multi-kingdom pathogen discovery in marine metagenomes, applicable to other coral disease systems. The results will critically inform targeted histological and molecular studies to confirm pathogenicity.
proposals.novelty: While subtraction is a known method, its systematic application to simultaneously interrogate the viral and eukaryotic components of the SCTLD metagenome, using a pipeline benchmarked on complex eukaryotic host genomes (inspired by Salzberg's human genome work), represents a novel and holistic approach to this specific disease etiology.

proposals.title: In Silico Virulence Profiling of Candidate Bacterial Pathogens in Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD)
proposals.motivation: Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) is a devastating, rapidly spreading disease threatening Caribbean coral reefs. While bacterial consortia are implicated, specific causative agents and their pathogenic mechanisms remain unclear, hindering targeted mitigation. Pre-2026, high-throughput metagenomic and genomic sequencing has identified candidate bacterial lineages, but their functional potential for virulence is largely uncharacterized.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in silico tools developed for bacterial pathogenicity prediction in clinical and agricultural settings can identify conserved and novel virulence factors and genomic islands within candidate SCTLD-associated bacterial genomes, pinpointing specific taxa and mechanisms with high pathogenic potential.
proposals.method: We will apply established, pre-2026 software suites (e.g., VirulenceFinder, VFDB, IslandViewer 4, PHASTER, and custom HMM-based pipelines) to a curated set of high-quality, closed bacterial genomes and metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) from SCTLD-affected and healthy coral samples. Analysis will include systematic annotation of known virulence factor genes (e.g., toxins, adhesins, secretion systems) and identification of horizontally acquired genomic islands. Comparative genomic analysis will contrast virulence profiles between disease-associated and healthy-sample bacteria to identify statistically enriched features.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive computational virulence profile for SCTLD candidate pathogens, moving beyond taxonomic identification to functional mechanism. It will prioritize specific bacterial strains and virulence modules for downstream experimental validation (e.g., in vitro assays, infection models) and inform the development of targeted diagnostic or intervention strategies.
proposals.novelty: This represents a novel adaptation of well-established clinical bacteriology bioinformatics tools to the understudied realm of marine coral disease, applying a robust genotype-to-phenotype prediction framework to a complex environmental microbiome challenge where classical Koch's postulates are difficult to fulfill.

proposals.title: Spatial mapping of host-microbe transcriptomic dynamics in Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) lesions using Visium HD
proposals.motivation: Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) is a devastating, rapidly spreading coral disease, but its precise pathogenic mechanism remains unclear. Pre-2026 studies suggest a microbial consortium is involved, yet conventional bulk or single-cell RNA-seq loses critical spatial context, preventing the localization of specific microbial activity and host immune response within the structured lesion microenvironment. Applying spatial transcriptomics, a technique revolutionizing medical pathology, to this ecological crisis can directly link microbial presence and function to tissue pathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatially resolved transcriptomics will reveal distinct, concentric zones of host immune dysregulation and metabolic stress within SCTLD lesions, directly colocalized with the expression of virulence genes from a core bacterial pathogen(s).
proposals.method: We will apply the 10x Genomics Visium HD Spatial Gene Expression platform to healthy coral and active SCTLD lesion samples. Our analysis pipeline will use the Space Ranger suite for alignment and feature counting. We will employ a dual-reference strategy: the *Acropora cervicornis* genome (GCF_022143615.1) for host reads and a custom microbial genome database (including known SCTLD-associated bacteria from pre-2026 studies) for pathogen identification. Spatial differential expression and colocalization analysis will be performed using Seurat and SPOTlight to deconvolve cell-type-specific signals and map microbial gene activity onto histological zones.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution spatial atlas of host-pathogen interaction in a coral disease, identifying the direct molecular actors at the disease front. It will move the field from correlative microbial surveys to causal, spatially explicit models of pathogenesis, providing specific targets for diagnostic and intervention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While spatial transcriptomics is established in oncology, its application to marine disease ecology is novel. This project innovatively adapts clinical-grade, dual-host-pathogen analytical frameworks to solve a critical problem in environmental genomics, moving beyond bulk community sequencing to mechanistic, spatially resolved understanding.

proposals.title: A Standardized Metagenomic Pipeline for Pan-Caribbean Surveillance of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD)
proposals.motivation: Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) is a devastating, rapidly spreading epizootic that has caused unprecedented mortality in Caribbean coral reefs since its emergence. Pre-2026 studies have identified a dysbiotic microbial consortium associated with lesions, but a comprehensive, standardized understanding of the disease's microbial etiology and spatial-temporal dynamics across the entire Caribbean basin is lacking, hindering coordinated management and mitigation efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deploying a standardized, high-throughput metagenomic sequencing and analysis pipeline across multiple Caribbean sites will reveal consistent, core microbial signatures of SCTLD, distinguish them from background environmental and healthy coral microbiome variation, and identify geographic or host-specific strain variants critical for understanding disease transmission.
proposals.method: We will adapt and scale the computational pipeline from our prior HIV-1 and human genome work for environmental metagenomics. The method involves: 1) Standardized sample collection (lesion, healthy, water) from partner institutions across 10+ Caribbean jurisdictions; 2) Whole-genome shotgun sequencing on Illumina platforms; 3) A dual analytical approach using Kraken2/Bracken for broad taxonomic profiling and de novo assembly (via metaSPAdes) with DIAMOND alignment to protein databases (e.g., NCBI-nr) for functional gene annotation; 4) Integrated analysis comparing SCTLD-associated microbial communities against healthy coral and environmental baselines, leveraging statistical frameworks from our phenotype-to-genotype work.
proposals.contribution: This project will establish the first open-access, pan-Caribbean metagenomic atlas for SCTLD, providing a critical resource for the coral research community. It will deliver a standardized, publicly available bioinformatics pipeline for environmental disease surveillance and generate specific, testable hypotheses about putative pathogenic agents and their genomic features, directly informing future intervention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While regional SCTLD microbiome studies exist, this proposal's novelty lies in its scale, standardization, and direct application of robust human pathogen surveillance methods (e.g., from our HIV-1 transcriptome work) to a complex environmental disease system, enabling direct cross-site comparisons previously not possible.

proposals.title: Systematic Re-evaluation of Predicted Protein-Coding Genes in Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) Affected Corals for Functional Non-Coding RNA Discovery
proposals.motivation: Current genome annotation pipelines, including those used for coral genomes, heavily prioritize protein-coding gene prediction, potentially misclassifying regions encoding functional non-coding RNAs (ncRNAs). This is critical in the study of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD), where differential gene expression analysis may overlook key regulatory ncRNAs involved in the coral's stress and immune response. A systematic re-evaluation is needed to accurately characterize the full functional repertoire of the coral genome.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant subset of genomic regions currently annotated as protein-coding genes in key coral species (e.g., *Orbicella faveolata*) in fact encode functional long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) or other structured ncRNAs, and that some of these are differentially expressed during SCTLD progression.
proposals.method: We will apply a multi-tool computational pipeline to re-annotate existing coral genome assemblies and matched RNA-seq datasets from SCTLD-infected and healthy samples. The pipeline will integrate: 1) PhyloCSF for evolutionary conservation signatures of protein coding, 2) CPAT and FEELnc for coding potential assessment, 3) Infernal for Rfam-based structured RNA detection, and 4) de novo assembly of transcripts using StringTie followed by classification with FEELnc. Differential expression analysis of re-classified loci will be performed using DESeq2.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a corrected and more comprehensive annotation of the functional genome for critical reef-building corals, specifically identifying ncRNAs associated with disease. It will provide a validated computational framework applicable to other non-model organisms. The results will refine the molecular understanding of coral stress response, identifying novel regulatory targets for SCTLD.
proposals.novelty: While ncRNA discovery is established in human genomics, its systematic application to resolve annotation ambiguities in coral genomes, particularly in the context of a devastating novel disease, is novel. This approach directly tests the default assumption of protein-coding function in existing coral gene catalogs.

proposals.title: Functional Validation of 210 Predicted Genes on Human Chromosome 2 Using CRISPR-Cas9 Knockout and Transcriptomic Profiling
proposals.motivation: The completion of the T2T-CHM13 human reference genome has revealed thousands of previously unannotated predicted genes, many of which lack any experimental validation of their biological function. This represents a significant gap in our understanding of the human genome, as these genes could encode novel proteins or regulatory elements relevant to health and disease. Systematic experimental testing is required to move these computational predictions into the catalog of known human biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant subset of the 210 predicted genes on chromosome 2 are functional, and that their disruption will lead to measurable phenotypic changes, such as altered cellular growth, morphology, or gene expression profiles, in relevant human cell lines.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize CRISPR-Cas9 guide RNA libraries targeting each of the 210 predicted genes. Using a lentiviral delivery system, we will perform pooled knockout screens in a panel of human cell lines (e.g., HEK293, HAP1). Phenotypic selection will be applied (e.g., proliferation, stress response), and guide abundance will be tracked via deep sequencing to identify genes essential under specific conditions. For hits, we will perform single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) on knockout clones to characterize transcriptomic consequences, linking gene disruption to specific pathway dysregulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale experimental dataset on the functionality of novel genes predicted from the complete human reference. It will validate or refute computational gene predictions, assign preliminary biological roles to new genes, and generate a publicly available resource of knockout cell lines and associated molecular phenotypes for the community.
proposals.novelty: While computational annotation is mature, systematic, high-throughput experimental validation of *de novo* predicted genes from the complete diploid genome remains rare. This project directly bridges the gap between the sequence-based discoveries of the T2T consortium and functional genomics, applying scalable perturbation methods used for known genes to the uncharted portion of the genome.

proposals.title: Adapting A+T-Rich Genome Gene Finders for Improved Annotation of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD)-Associated Microbes
proposals.motivation: Accurate gene annotation is critical for understanding the functional genomics of microbes associated with Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD), a devastating Caribbean outbreak. Many coral-associated microbes, including viruses and bacteria, have A+T-rich genomes, which challenge standard gene prediction tools optimized for model organisms. This gap hinders the functional analysis of metagenomic and whole-genome sequencing data from SCTLD studies, limiting our ability to identify virulence factors and metabolic pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: Gene prediction algorithms specifically developed for A+T-rich nuclear genomes, such as those of Dictyostelium discoideum and Arabidopsis thaliana, can be adapted and optimized to significantly improve the sensitivity and accuracy of gene annotation in A+T-rich microbial genomes relevant to coral disease.
proposals.method: We will compile a benchmark set of A+T-rich microbial genomes from SCTLD-affected and healthy coral samples, using existing pre-2026 sequencing data. We will adapt and retrain established ab initio gene finders like GlimmerHMM (inspired by Arabidopsis) and GeneMark-ES/ET, initially developed for A+T-rich eukaryotes, for prokaryotic and viral contexts. The core innovation involves retraining these models on high-quality, manually curated gene sets from our target microbial clades, integrating ribosomal binding site models for prokaryotes, and adjusting for shorter intergenic regions. Performance will be benchmarked against standard tools (e.g., Prodigal) using RNA-seq transcriptome data from the same samples for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, optimized computational pipeline for gene prediction in non-model, A+T-rich microbial genomes, directly applicable to coral disease research. It will generate more reliable genome annotations for SCTLD-associated pathogens, enabling more accurate downstream analyses of protein function, virulence, and host-pathogen interactions. The tools and models will be made publicly available to enhance genomic studies of other A+T-rich environmental microbes.
proposals.novelty: While gene finders exist for standard bacteria and viruses, the targeted adaptation of algorithms from A+T-rich nuclear genomes to the specific context of coral-associated microbes is novel. This approach directly addresses a known technical bottleneck in marine disease genomics by leveraging pre-2026 computational biology methods in a new ecological application.

proposals.title: Targeted Capture and Population Genomics of Hypervariable *rifin* Loci in *Plasmodium falciparum*
proposals.motivation: The *rifin* gene family in *Plasmodium falciparum* is crucial for antigenic variation and immune evasion, but its extreme diversity and repetitive nature make it intractable for standard whole-genome sequencing (WGS) in population studies. Current WGS approaches, while powerful for core genome analysis, fail to assemble or accurately genotype these hypervariable subregions, leaving a critical gap in understanding parasite diversity and transmission dynamics. A method to specifically and cost-effectively sequence these loci from many clinical samples is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that designing a custom capture panel targeting conserved flanking sequences of *rifin* loci will enable high-coverage sequencing of these hypervariable regions from hundreds of parasite genomes, revealing previously hidden population structure and selection pressures.
proposals.method: We will design biotinylated RNA baits (Agilent SureSelect) targeting conserved sequences flanking *rifin* open reading frames, based on the 3D7 reference genome and known variants. DNA from hundreds of diverse *P. falciparum* clinical isolates will be enriched using this panel and sequenced on the Illumina platform. We will develop a specialized bioinformatics pipeline using the SPAdes assembler for targeted loci and a custom variant caller to handle the highly repetitive and multi-allelic nature of the data, with validation via long-read sequencing on a subset of samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution population genomics dataset focused on the *rifin* repertoire, enabling analysis of recombination, selection, and strain diversity. It will provide a scalable, cost-effective template for studying other complex, hypervariable gene families in pathogens, moving beyond the limitations of standard WGS for critical antigenic determinants.
proposals.novelty: While targeted capture is established, its application to deconvolve the complex *rifin* superfamily for population genomics is novel. This approach moves beyond reference-dependent alignment to *de novo* assembly of hypervariable regions across a population, a strategy not yet applied at scale to this problem.

proposals.title: A High-Quality, Telomere-to-Telomere Reference Genome for Anopheles gambiae Using Adaptive, A+T-Rich Sequencing Techniques
proposals.motivation: Anopheles mosquitoes are the primary vectors for malaria, a disease causing hundreds of thousands of deaths annually. Despite the existence of reference genomes, the highly repetitive, A+T-rich regions, particularly centromeres and telomeres, remain poorly assembled due to technological biases in standard sequencing. These regions are critical for understanding chromosome biology, insecticide resistance gene regulation, and vector population genomics. Pre-2026 advances in generating complete human and coral genomes highlight the need for similar high-quality references for major disease vectors.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting and optimizing long-read sequencing and assembly methods specifically for A+T-rich heterochromatic regions, we can generate the first complete, gap-free, and fully phased diploid genome assembly for Anopheles gambiae, accurately resolving its most challenging repetitive structures.
proposals.method: We will apply and refine techniques from recent complete diploid genome projects, such as those for human and coral, to Anopheles. This involves using ultra-long Oxford Nanopore reads and high-fidelity (HiFi) PacBio reads, with library preparations optimized for extreme base composition. We will employ the Shasta assembler and the Verkko telomere-to-telomere pipeline, adapting their parameters and error-correction steps for A+T bias. Assembly validation will use Hi-C data for scaffolding and the Mercury quality assessment toolkit to benchmark against existing references like AgamP4.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new gold-standard reference genome for Anopheles gambiae, enabling the precise mapping of genetic variants linked to insecticide resistance and vector competence. It will provide the community with a complete genomic context for functional studies of heterochromatin and serve as a benchmark for sequencing other non-model organisms with similarly challenging genomes.
proposals.novelty: While complete genomes exist for humans and model organisms, this proposal specifically tailors the cutting-edge, A+T-optimized assembly methods—developed for organisms like coral (SCTLD research context)—to a major disease vector, moving beyond the standard 'healthy' reference to fully resolve its most problematic genomic architecture.

proposals.title: Metatranscriptomic Profiling of Viral and Eukaryotic Pathogens in Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) Lesions
proposals.motivation: Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) is a catastrophic, ongoing epizootic devastating coral reefs across the Caribbean. While bacterial consortia have been implicated, the potential roles of viral and eukaryotic (e.g., fungal, protozoan) pathogens remain poorly characterized. A comprehensive, unbiased survey of the non-bacterial microbiome in diseased versus healthy coral tissue is critical for identifying potential causative or contributing agents, as pre-2026 studies have not definitively resolved the disease's etiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SCTLD lesions harbor distinct viral and eukaryotic microbial communities compared to healthy coral tissue, and that one or more specific viral or eukaryotic taxa are consistently associated with active disease lesions.
proposals.method: We will apply a refined version of the pipeline used for 'Comprehensive Transcriptome Annotation of Thousands of HIV-1 Genomes' to existing and newly generated whole-genome and metatranscriptomic sequencing data from SCTLD-affected and healthy corals. Reads will be aligned to a comprehensive reference database including the complete diploid coral genome (PacBio HiFi), known coral viruses, and eukaryotic pathogen genomes. Unaligned reads will be assembled de novo using metaSPAdes, and contigs will be analyzed via DIAMOND for protein homology and BLASTn for taxonomic assignment to identify novel viral and eukaryotic sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic catalog of viral and eukaryotic associates of SCTLD, directly testing their potential pathogenic role. It will generate a public resource of annotated microbial sequences for the coral disease research community. The findings will critically inform future experimental and ecological studies aimed at mitigating this disease.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond the predominant bacterial focus in SCTLD research by leveraging advanced, scalable annotation pipelines from viral genomics to conduct a holistic survey of understudied pathogen kingdoms. The integration of a high-quality coral host genome reference enables more sensitive detection of foreign eukaryotic transcripts.

proposals.title: Integrating Metagenomic Signatures with Oceanographic Data to Model the Spatiotemporal Transmission Pathways of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD)
proposals.motivation: Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) is a devastating, rapidly spreading marine epidemic threatening Atlantic and Caribbean coral reefs. While microbial consortia are implicated in the disease, the precise environmental drivers and transmission pathways remain poorly understood, hindering management and mitigation efforts. Integrating high-resolution genomic data with physical oceanographic models represents a critical, unexplored approach to predicting disease spread.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatiotemporal spread of SCTLD is driven by the interaction of specific, detectable metagenomic signatures in coral holobionts and seawater with oceanographic currents and environmental parameters, enabling the construction of predictive transmission models.
proposals.method: We will apply whole-genome and metagenomic sequencing to paired healthy and SCTLD-affected coral tissue and seawater samples across a spatiotemporal gradient in the Florida Reef Tract and Caribbean. We will identify disease-associated microbial and viral signatures using differential abundance analysis (e.g., DESeq2, LEfSe). These signatures will be integrated as covariates with high-resolution oceanographic current data (HYCOM) and satellite-derived environmental data (SST, chlorophyll) within a Bayesian phylogeographic and machine learning framework (e.g., using RStan or a spatial random forest model) to infer and predict dispersal pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially explicit, mechanism-informed model of SCTLD transmission, identifying key environmental corridors for spread. It will deliver a validated toolkit combining metagenomics and geospatial analysis for marine disease ecology. The findings will provide actionable intelligence for targeted reef surveillance and intervention.
proposals.novelty: While metagenomic studies of SCTLD exist and ocean models are used for larval dispersal, the direct integration of high-resolution genomic signatures as dynamic tracers within physical transmission models is a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond correlation to mechanistic, predictive modeling of a marine disease.

proposals.title: Robust Omnitig Discovery in Sequence Graphs with Real-World Sequencing Errors
proposals.motivation: De Bruijn and variation graphs are fundamental for genome assembly and variant analysis, but their theoretical guarantees, like the omnitig safety condition, assume error-free input. Real sequencing data from diverse projects, such as coral disease (SCTLD) or viral (ZIKV) surveillance, contains errors that manifest as spurious edges and bubbles, corrupting graph topology and misleading downstream analyses like transcriptome annotation and phenotype-to-genotype mapping. A theory that accounts for these errors is critical for reliable biological inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling sequencing errors as a probabilistic or weighted graph perturbation, we can redefine and prove a robust omnitig safety condition that guarantees the recovery of true genomic paths even in the presence of erroneous edges.
proposals.method: We will extend the omnitig theory by integrating error profiles from real sequencing platforms (e.g., Illumina, PacBio) into the graph model. First, we will assign likelihoods to edges based on read support and quality scores, treating low-support edges as potential errors. We will then adapt the omnitig algorithm to operate on this weighted graph, defining a new safety condition where a path is safe if all its constituent omnitigs remain consistent under all edge deletions below a statistically defined error threshold. We will validate the method using simulated error-injected graphs and real datasets from human diploid benchmarks and viral genomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal safety guarantee for graph traversal algorithms applied to noisy, real-world sequencing data. It will directly improve the accuracy of genome assembly, variant detection, and transcriptome annotation pipelines by making them robust to sequencing artifacts. The resulting algorithms and theoretical framework will be disseminated as open-source software integrated with existing graph toolkits.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods heuristically clean or correct graphs post-hoc, our approach is novel in formally integrating an error model directly into the foundational combinatorial theory of safe walks, providing provable guarantees rather than ad-hoc fixes.

proposals.title: Benchmarking the Omnitig Algorithm for De Novo Assembly on Real, Error-Prone Genomic Sequencing Data
proposals.motivation: De novo assembly of genomes from short-read sequencing data is foundational to genomics but remains computationally challenging, especially for complex or novel organisms. While unitig-based assemblers are standard, the recently proposed omnitig algorithm offers a theoretically optimal, linear-time solution for constructing safe, longer contigs in the presence of errors. Its practical performance on real, noisy datasets—such as those from environmental samples, degraded tissues, or viral quasispecies—has not been systematically evaluated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the omnitig algorithm will produce more contiguous and accurate assemblies than standard unitig-based methods when applied to real, error-prone genomic datasets, without a prohibitive increase in computational resource use.
proposals.method: We will implement the omnitig algorithm within a robust assembly pipeline and benchmark it against established unitig-based assemblers (e.g., SPAdes, MEGAHIT). We will use diverse, pre-2026 real datasets, including coral metagenomes affected by Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD), Zika virus (ZIKV) clinical isolates, and human diploid genome sequencing data with varying coverage and error profiles. Performance will be measured using standard metrics (N50, number of contigs, misassembly rate) against high-quality reference genomes where available, and via alignment-free methods for novel sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, empirical validation of the omnitig algorithm's utility for practical genome assembly. It will deliver a benchmarked software tool and guidelines for its application to complex sequencing projects, directly benefiting research in pathogen surveillance, environmental genomics, and personalized medicine where data quality is often suboptimal.
proposals.novelty: While the omnitig algorithm's theoretical advantages are known, its systematic evaluation on real-world, noisy sequencing data—a core challenge in Salzberg's recent work on HIV, coral, and human genomes—is novel and addresses a critical gap between theory and application.

proposals.title: A Phylogenomic and Reassortment-Aware Model for Antigenic Drift Prediction in Influenza Vaccine Strain Selection
proposals.motivation: Annual influenza vaccine formulation remains a reactive process, relying on antigenic characterization of circulating strains that lags behind viral evolution. This delay can lead to suboptimal vaccine efficacy when significant antigenic drift occurs after strain selection. The integration of large-scale genomic surveillance data with predictive models offers a promising avenue for more proactive selection, but current methods often treat phylogeny and reassortment separately.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a formal model integrating whole-genome phylogenetic signals with segment-specific reassortment networks will outperform models based on single-gene (e.g., HA) phylogenies in predicting the emergence of antigenically novel influenza virus clades.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline building on established whole-genome alignment and variant calling methods (e.g., from the T2T human genome benchmark work). The core model will combine a whole-genome maximum-likelihood phylogeny with a time-scaled network analysis of reassortment events inferred from segment incongruence. We will train and test the model using historical global influenza surveillance data (pre-2026) from GISAID, correlating predicted emerging clades with subsequent antigenic cartography data from HI assays. Feature importance will be assessed to identify genomic markers beyond HA1.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, publicly available software tool for the vaccine community that quantifies the risk of antigenic drift from genomic data. It will also deliver a validated framework demonstrating the added predictive value of whole-genome and reassortment data, moving beyond heuristic rules used in strain selection meetings.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic methods are standard, the explicit, simultaneous integration of whole-genome evolutionary history and reassortment network topology into a predictive model for antigenic drift is a novel synthesis. This approach leverages the full genomic context, akin to the comprehensive annotation principles applied in prior work on HIV-1 and human genomes, but for a dynamic, segmented virus.

proposals.title: Modeling the Spatial Dynamics of Viral Reassortment Using Commodity Flow Networks: A Case Study in Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD)
proposals.motivation: Understanding the geographic pathways of viral spread and genetic mixing is critical for predicting outbreaks and designing interventions, as seen in arboviruses like Zika (ZIKV). Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) presents a complex, environmentally-driven epidemic where viral-like agents may be involved, but the spatial mechanisms of pathogen dispersal and potential genomic reassortment across reef systems remain poorly characterized. Existing phylogeographic models often lack explicit integration of real-world movement constraints and capacities.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial spread and genomic reassortment patterns of viral sequences associated with marine disease outbreaks, such as SCTLD, can be accurately modeled and predicted by adapting commodity flow optimization frameworks from trade logistics, which account for source-sink dynamics and transport limitations.
proposals.method: We will adapt the classic transportation problem and gravity models from operations research to model pathogen flow between reef sites. Using pre-2026 whole-genome and transcriptome sequencing data from healthy and SCTLD-affected coral samples, we will construct a reference catalog of viral gene segments. Network parameters (e.g., 'supply' of viral variants, 'demand' at susceptible sites, ‘transport cost’ based on oceanographic distance and current data) will be estimated. The model will be optimized to minimize a cost function of spatial spread, and its predictions for genomic mixing will be tested against observed reassortment patterns using phylogenetic comparative analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for predicting the spatial spread and genetic evolution of environmental pathogens, directly applicable to SCTLD and other wildlife diseases. It will deliver a new software tool for spatial genomic analysis that integrates logistical constraints, extending beyond traditional phylogenetic diffusion models. The results could inform targeted surveillance and containment strategies for marine epidemics.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying well-established commodity flow and logistics optimization models, common in trade analysis, to the problem of viral genomic geography and reassortment, a crossover not previously explored in genomic epidemiology. It moves beyond correlation-based landscape genetics to a capacity-constrained dispersal model.

proposals.title: A Scalable, Automated Pipeline for Real-Time Genomic Surveillance of Emerging Pathogens and Environmental Samples
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic demonstrated the critical need for rapid genomic surveillance to track pathogen evolution and inform public health responses. Similarly, environmental crises like Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) require fast genomic analysis of coral samples to understand disease dynamics. Current workflows for whole-genome sequencing and analysis are fragmented and slow, creating a bottleneck for timely intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating automated sample processing, cloud-based high-performance computing, and rapid phylogenetic placement algorithms into a single, end-to-end pipeline will enable near-real-time genomic surveillance, significantly reducing the time from sample collection to actionable phylogenetic insights.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular pipeline leveraging the DRAGEN platform on AWS for accelerated secondary analysis (alignment, variant calling, and de novo assembly). For rapid phylogenetic placement, we will adapt and scale the UShER algorithm, proven for SARS-CoV-2, to create dynamic reference trees for other pathogens (e.g., ZIKV) and environmental metagenomes. The system will be containerized using Nextflow for portability and automated to trigger analysis upon sequencing completion, with results visualized via an interactive dashboard.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, open-source software framework that operationalizes genomic surveillance, moving it from a batch-processed research activity to a continuous monitoring tool. It will provide a benchmarked model for applying real-time genomics to both clinical pathogens and environmental samples like corals, directly addressing needs highlighted during recent outbreaks and ecological events.
proposals.novelty: While individual components exist, the novelty lies in the fully automated integration of high-speed sequencing analysis with instantaneous phylogenetic placement into a single, scalable pipeline designed for multiple, disparate use cases (human health and environmental monitoring) under a unified architecture.

proposals.title: Predicting Influenza A Virus Reassortment Potential from Segment-Specific Genomic Compatibility Signatures
proposals.motivation: Viral reassortment, particularly in influenza A, is a major source of pandemic strains, but predicting which co-infecting lineages are likely to reassort remains a significant challenge. Current methods rely on phylogenetic analysis post-emergence. A predictive, sequence-based model of segment compatibility would be a transformative tool for genomic surveillance and pandemic risk assessment, allowing for proactive monitoring of circulating strains with high reassortment potential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that influenza A virus genomic segments possess lineage-specific sequence features and interaction signatures that govern their compatibility, and that these features can be learned computationally to predict the likelihood of reassortment between any two given parental strains.
proposals.method: We will curate a comprehensive dataset of known reassortant and parental influenza A genomes from public databases (GISAID, NCBI). Using a reference-based alignment pipeline, we will extract segment-specific k-mer spectra, codon usage biases, and predicted RNA secondary structure features. We will train a machine learning model, specifically a gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost), to classify segment pairs as compatible or incompatible, integrating features from all eight segments to predict whole-genome reassortment outcomes. Model performance will be validated on held-out lineages and simulated reassortment scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first generalizable, sequence-based predictive model for influenza reassortment, moving the field from descriptive phylogenetics to proactive forecasting. It will deliver a publicly available software tool for scoring reassortment risk between user-provided genomes, directly enhancing global surveillance efforts for pandemic influenza.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have identified specific molecular constraints post-hoc, this approach is novel in its comprehensive, machine-learning-driven search for predictive compatibility rules across all segments simultaneously, framed as a direct prediction problem from sequence data alone.

proposals.title: Integrating Intron Evolution with Gene Function: A Comparative Genomic and Machine Learning Framework
proposals.motivation: Current models of intron evolution often treat intron gain and loss as neutral processes independent of gene sequence and function, a simplification that may obscure key evolutionary drivers. This gap limits our understanding of how functional constraints shape genome architecture, particularly in non-model organisms like corals where rapid environmental adaptation is critical. Linking intron dynamics to gene function could reveal new principles of genome evolution with implications for understanding disease (e.g., SCTLD in corals) and viral (e.g., ZIKV) adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intron presence, density, and evolutionary stability are statistically associated with specific gene functional categories and protein sequence features, and that these associations can be modeled to predict functional constraints on genome structure.
proposals.method: We will construct a large-scale comparative genomic dataset from diverse eukaryotes, including coral and human genomes, using established reference-based pipelines (e.g., HISAT2, StringTie) for consistent annotation. For each gene, we will compute features including functional category (GO, Pfam), protein domain architecture, and evolutionary rate (dN/dS). We will train a gradient-boosted tree model (XGBoost) to predict intron presence/density from these features, controlling for phylogenetic history. Statistical significance of associations will be assessed using permutation tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a predictive model linking gene function to intron architecture, moving beyond descriptive correlations. It will deliver a new, publicly available benchmark dataset of functionally annotated gene structures across species. The findings will provide a framework for interpreting intron evolution in the context of phenotypic adaptation and stress response in systems like coral health.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its explicit integration of protein-level functional constraints into a predictive model of intron evolution, relaxing the standard assumption of independence. It applies advanced machine learning methods, common in personalized genomics, to the classical problem of intron evolution in a large-scale comparative framework.

proposals.title: A Graph-Based Genome Alignment Framework for Conserved Intron Position Mapping Across Diverged Eukaryotes
proposals.motivation: Intron positions are key evolutionary markers for understanding gene structure evolution and functional genomics, but their conservation is difficult to map accurately across highly diverged species using standard linear reference alignments. Pre-2026, the field has advanced with pangenome graphs (e.g., minigraph, minigraph-cactus) for structural variant analysis, but these have not been systematically applied to the specific problem of intron position conservation, especially in non-model organisms relevant to Salzberg's work like corals or pathogens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a genome graph alignment approach, which incorporates population-level variation and structural diversity into the reference, will enable more sensitive and accurate identification of conserved intron positions across deep evolutionary distances than linear reference alignment.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the minigraph-cactus pipeline, a pre-2026 graph alignment framework, to create a multi-species genome graph for target clades (e.g., anthozoans or flaviviruses). We will develop a novel annotation module that projects high-confidence intron annotations from well-annotated genomes (like human or model organisms) onto the graph and traces their paths through alignments of other species. The analysis will use the GraphAligner tool for mapping and custom scripts to extract and score intron position conservation from the graph's variation-aware alignments.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new computational method and software for evolutionary intron analysis, a high-confidence set of deeply conserved intron positions for chosen clades, and a benchmark demonstrating superior performance over linear methods. It will directly contribute to studies of gene structure evolution in Salzberg's research areas, such as coral (SCTLD) and viral (ZIKV) genomics.
proposals.novelty: While graph genomes are used for structural variation, applying them specifically as a tool for evolutionary tracing of a fundamental gene feature (intron positions) across deep phylogenies is a novel synthesis. The approach moves beyond pairwise or multiple sequence alignment to a pangenomic framework for this classic problem.

proposals.title: Predicting Intron Gain and Loss from Genomic Sequence Features Using Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Intron gain and loss are fundamental evolutionary processes shaping eukaryotic genomes, yet their underlying mechanisms and predictive sequence signatures remain poorly understood. Current comparative genomics methods identify intron presence/absence but cannot predict these events from primary sequence alone, limiting our ability to infer gene structure evolution in non-model organisms like corals or emerging pathogens like Zika virus (ZIKV).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that local genomic sequence features, including motifs, structural properties, and repeat content, contain sufficient information to predict intron presence or absence at specific loci, enabling the classification of intron gain and loss events.
proposals.method: We will train supervised classification models (e.g., gradient boosting machines like XGBoost and convolutional neural networks) on aligned genomic loci from diverse eukaryotic genomes, including human, coral, and viral datasets. Features will include k-mer frequencies, splice site scores, GC content, and repetitive element annotations derived from pre-2026 tools. Models will be benchmarked against a curated reference set of known intron gain/loss events from comparative genomics studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational framework for predicting intron dynamics directly from sequence, advancing beyond descriptive cataloging. It will offer a new tool for annotating challenging genomes, such as those of corals affected by stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD), and for analyzing the evolution of viral and pathogen genomes where intron content can influence phenotype.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning is used for splice site prediction, its application to the evolutionary problem of intron gain/loss prediction from raw sequence is novel. This approach leverages Salzberg's expertise in genome analysis and sequence classification to address a core question in genome biology with a methodology distinct from existing phylogenetic comparisons.

proposals.title: Leveraging Protein Language Model Embeddings to Predict Pathogenic Splice-Altering Variants in Human Disease
