proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of splice-altering variants (SAVs) remains a critical challenge in genomic medicine, as current tools rely heavily on nucleotide-level sequence features and conservation scores, often missing variants with subtle effects. Protein language models (pLMs) like ESM-2 and ProtT5, trained on millions of protein sequences, encode deep evolutionary constraints on protein structure and function that are not directly captured by DNA-centric models. Incorporating these protein-level constraints could significantly improve the identification of pathogenic SAVs, especially in non-canonical splice regions, advancing the interpretation of whole-genome sequencing data in clinical and research contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embeddings from protein language models, which encapsulate evolutionary and structural constraints, will provide complementary predictive power to existing genomic and conservation features, leading to a more accurate and generalizable classifier for pathogenic splice-altering variants.
proposals.method: We will train a supervised machine learning model (e.g., XGBoost or a neural network) to classify known pathogenic and benign SAVs from databases like ClinVar and gnomAD. Input features will combine: 1) per-residue embeddings extracted from a pre-trained pLM (ESM-2) for the wild-type and variant protein sequences, capturing altered evolutionary profiles; 2) established genomic features from tools like SpliceAI and MMSplice; and 3) conservation scores. We will benchmark this integrated model against state-of-the-art splice prediction tools using held-out test sets and novel variants from the T2T-CHM13 diploid genome benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available variant effect predictor that explicitly integrates protein evolutionary constraints into splice site prediction. It will provide a deeper mechanistic understanding of how protein-level selection pressure influences RNA splicing, and deliver a practical tool for prioritizing pathogenic variants in undiagnosed genetic diseases and in complex genomic studies, such as those of coral (SCTLD) or viral (ZIKV) systems.
proposals.novelty: While pLMs have been used for protein structure and function prediction, their application as direct input features for discriminating DNA-level splicing variants is novel. This approach moves beyond the standard paradigm of using pLMs for protein-specific tasks, instead leveraging their embedded evolutionary knowledge to inform a core DNA sequence interpretation problem.

proposals.title: Interpretable Deep Learning for Splice Site Prediction: Deploying XAI to Discover Novel Regulatory Motifs in Genomic Sequences
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models like Splam have achieved state-of-the-art accuracy in splice site prediction, but their 'black-box' nature limits biological insight. Identifying the specific sequence motifs that drive these predictions is crucial for understanding splicing regulation, which is implicated in diseases like cancer and Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD). Pre-2026, most motif discovery relies on pre-defined models or simple attribution methods, failing to fully leverage the complex patterns learned by modern neural networks.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying advanced post-hoc explainable AI (XAI) methods to Splam will reveal novel, high-confidence sequence motifs influencing splice site predictions, motifs that are not captured by traditional position weight matrices or simpler interpretability techniques.
proposals.method: We will apply a suite of post-hoc XAI methods, including DeepLIFT and Integrated Gradients, to the pre-trained Splam model to generate per-nucleotide importance scores across diverse genomic sequences (e.g., human, coral, viral). These saliency maps will be clustered and used as input to a motif discovery algorithm like TF-MoDISco (pre-2026) to identify de novo sequence patterns. We will validate motifs by assessing their enrichment in known splice regions and testing their predictive power in simplified logistic models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a catalog of novel, model-derived motifs that govern splice site recognition, moving beyond sequence conservation to functional model drivers. It will provide a framework for interpreting complex genomic deep learning models, accelerating the transition from phenotype to genotype in studies of splicing variation in health, disease, and environmental stress responses in non-model organisms.
proposals.novelty: While XAI is used in computer vision, its systematic application for de novo motif discovery in a high-performance splice site model is novel. This approach moves beyond verifying known motifs to actively discovering new regulatory elements learned by the model from large-scale genomic data.

proposals.title: Splam-Genome: Ultra-Fast, Whole-Genome Annotation of Splice Sites Using Optimized Deep Learning
proposals.motivation: Accurate splice site annotation is fundamental for interpreting genomic variants, especially in disease studies and personalized genomics. Current tools are too slow for routine, whole-genome scoring, creating a bottleneck in large-scale analyses like population genomics or rapid phenotype-to-genotype workflows. Enabling minute-scale scoring of every potential splice site in a genome would revolutionize the speed and scope of genomic discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Splam deep learning model can be algorithmically optimized and computationally refactored for massively parallel, batched execution, enabling accurate scoring of all ~200 million potential splice sites in a human genome in under ten minutes.
proposals.method: We will optimize the existing Splam convolutional neural network architecture for batched, whole-genome inference. This involves implementing a custom, memory-efficient data loader for streaming genomic windows, refactoring the model in PyTorch to leverage tensor operations across millions of sites simultaneously, and integrating GPU-accelerated sequence encoding. We will implement a novel caching and indexing strategy for the reference genome to minimize I/O overhead, parallelizing the scoring pipeline using a distributed task queue (e.g., Celery) for cloud or cluster deployment.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a production-ready software tool that performs comprehensive splice site annotation at an unprecedented speed, moving from hours/days to minutes. It will provide a critical resource for large-scale genomic projects, such as the Human Pangenome Reference Consortium, and enable real-time integration of splice site scoring into clinical and research pipelines for variant interpretation.
proposals.novelty: While Splam itself is a recent advance in accuracy, the proposed system-level optimization for whole-genome, batched scoring is novel. Existing splice site predictors are not engineered for this scale and speed, making rapid, genome-wide scoring a previously unmet computational challenge.

proposals.title: A Scaffold-First Assembly Framework for Complex Genomes Using Long-Range Mapping and Short-Read Polish
proposals.motivation: Current de novo assembly paradigms, which rely on assembling short or long reads into contigs before scaffolding, struggle with highly repetitive, diploid, or structurally complex genomes like corals affected by Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) or diverse viral quasispecies. These methods often produce fragmented assemblies and miss large-scale structural variants critical for understanding disease mechanisms and organismal biology. A new approach is needed to directly capture large-scale genome architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that initiating genome assembly from long-range mapping data (e.g., Hi-C, optical maps) to first define megabase-scale scaffolds and haplotype blocks, followed by precise short-read sequence infusion, will produce more complete, accurate, and haplotype-resolved assemblies for complex genomes than read-first methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel assembly pipeline, 'Scaffold-First Assembler (SFA)'. First, we will use Hi-C data (processed with tools like Juicer) and Bionano optical maps to generate consensus physical scaffolds and partition reads by haplotype. These scaffolds, represented as consensus maps with estimated gap sizes, will serve as the primary assembly backbone. We will then use ultra-accurate short reads (e.g., Illumina) and a novel graph-based alignment algorithm to 'paint' sequence into these scaffolds, resolving gaps and polishing base calls. The method will be benchmarked against existing assemblers (e.g., Canu, HiFiasm) using pre-2026 diploid human genome standards and applied to novel coral and Zika virus (ZIKV) population datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly available software framework for genome assembly that prioritizes large-scale structural accuracy. It will provide benchmarked, haplotype-resolved assemblies for critical non-model organisms (corals) and viral populations, directly supporting genotype-to-phenotype studies in Salzberg's recent research themes. The method will set a new standard for assembling complex regions where current methods fail.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the canonical assembly workflow by treating long-range contact data as the primary source of assembly structure rather than a supplementary scaffolding step. It is non-obvious because it challenges the fundamental assumption that nucleotide sequence must be resolved before large-scale structure can be inferred.

proposals.title: A Complete Haploid Reference Genome of Podocarpus lawrencei Enables High-Resolution Analysis of Conifer Microbiome Dysbiosis Under Stress
proposals.motivation: Understanding host-microbiome interactions is critical for plant health, but studies in long-lived conifers are hampered by the lack of complete, haploid reference genomes, which are essential for accurately filtering host DNA from metagenomic samples. Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) research has demonstrated the power of precise host sequence removal to reveal microbial dysbiosis, a paradigm yet to be rigorously applied to forest pathogens. This project addresses this gap by creating a definitive genomic toolkit for a model conifer to dissect how environmental stress disrupts its native microbial community.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that disease in conifers is driven by a quantifiable dysbiosis of the native microbiome, and that a complete haploid host genome is necessary to achieve the sequencing depth and analytical precision required to identify the specific microbial taxa and host genetic pathways involved in this destabilization.
proposals.method: We will generate a high-quality, chromosome-scale haploid reference genome for Podocarpus lawrencei (the smallest conifer) using PacBio HiFi long-read sequencing and Hi-C chromatin capture, assembled with the Verkko or Hifiasm pipelines. We will then collect metagenomic samples from healthy and stressed individuals, using the host genome with BBSplit and Kraken2 for ultra-precise host sequence removal. Subsequent microbiome analysis will employ MetaPhlAn for profiling and HUMAnN for functional pathway analysis to characterize dysbiosis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first complete haploid genome for a model conifer, a critical community resource. It will establish a novel framework for plant-microbiome studies by demonstrating how a definitive host reference enables high-resolution metagenomic analysis, directly testing the dysbiosis-driven disease model. The results will identify candidate host genes and microbial consortia linked to resilience, informing future breeding and conservation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While host filtering is standard, using a purpose-built, complete haploid genome for a non-model plant to achieve near-perfect subtraction for dysbiosis analysis is novel. This approach transplants the analytical rigor from human genomics (e.g., T2T-CHM13) and coral disease studies into forest ecology, creating a new model system for host-microbiome genetics.

proposals.title: Linking Low-Abundance Coral Microbiome Members to Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) Pathogenesis via Culturomics and Functional Assays
proposals.motivation: Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) is a devastating, rapidly spreading disease threatening Caribbean reefs, but its precise microbial etiology remains unclear. Pre-2026 metagenomic studies have identified dysbiosis and candidate pathogens, yet causal links for low-abundance, hard-to-culture taxa are unproven, hindering targeted intervention. This gap mirrors challenges in human microbiome research, where associating rare microbes with disease requires deep sequencing and functional validation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, low-abundance bacterial taxa, detectable only through deep metagenomic sequencing of a large SCTLD cohort, are pathogenic drivers of disease, and that their isolation and characterization will reveal direct cytotoxic or immune-modulatory mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will perform deep (~100M paired-end reads/sample) shotgun metagenomic sequencing on a prospectively collected cohort of 500 coral samples (healthy, diseased, lesion interface) from the Florida Reef Tract, integrating data with coral transcriptomic and environmental metadata. Microbial signatures will be identified using our established, reference-based pipeline (Kraken2/Bracken) and association testing (MaAsLin2). Candidate taxa will be isolated using targeted, multi-medium culturomics from fresh samples. Isolates will be co-cultured with primary coral epithelial cell lines and immune cells (e.g., amoebocytes) to assay for cytotoxicity, apoptosis, and inflammatory cytokine release, validating pathogenic function.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond correlation to establish causal candidates for SCTLD, providing the first functionally characterized isolates of putative low-abundance pathogens. It will deliver a high-resolution, cohort-validated microbial map of the disease and establish a robust framework (deep sequencing → targeted culturomics → ex vivo validation) for linking low-biomass microbes to host pathology in complex environmental samples.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of deep cohort-scale metagenomics with targeted, hypothesis-driven culturomics and functional assays in coral model systems, a synthesis not yet applied to SCTLD. This approach moves decisively from sequence-based association to mechanistic insight for rare community members.

proposals.title: Phased host genome assemblies and deep metagenomes from long-read sequencing reveal associations between repetitive element variation and the microbiome in a large inflammatory disease cohort
proposals.motivation: The human microbiome is implicated in numerous diseases, but its interplay with the host genome, particularly the vast repetitive 'dark matter' (e.g., endogenous retroviruses, satellites), remains poorly understood. Short-read sequencing fails to resolve these complex repeats and often misses low-abundance microbial taxa. A unified long-read approach can simultaneously generate complete host haplotypes and sensitive metagenomic profiles, enabling a novel systems-level investigation of host-microbe interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structural variation and transcriptional activity of specific host repetitive elements are statistically associated with the presence and abundance of particular microbial taxa, and that these associations are enriched in specific inflammatory disease phenotypes compared to healthy controls.
proposals.method: We will apply PacBio HiFi and Oxford Nanopore sequencing to DNA and RNA from a well-phenotyped cohort of 500 patients with inflammatory bowel disease and 500 matched healthy controls. For each sample, we will perform *de novo* phased assembly using the Verkko or hifiasm assembler to create diploid genomes, followed by repeat annotation with RepeatMasker and Tandem Repeats Finder. Concurrently, we will profile the microbiome using MetaPhlAn and Kraken2 on long reads, validated with EMU for abundance estimation. Associations will be tested using multivariate regression (MaAsLin2) and custom algorithms correlating repeat copy number/expression with microbial abundance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale resource of phased, high-quality diploid genomes with matched deep metagenomes, directly testing links between the repetitive genome and the microbiome. It will establish a novel analytical framework for integrated host-microbe genomics and identify potential mechanistic links between repetitive element biology and dysbiosis in human disease.
proposals.novelty: The simultaneous generation of high-quality host assemblies and metagenomes from the same long-read data is novel, moving beyond correlation to enable direct, phased analysis of host repetitive regions and their co-occurrence with microbial communities, which has not been feasible at scale.

proposals.title: A Deep-Sequencing Metagenomic Analysis of a Large Cohort to Identify Low-Abundance Microbial Associates of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD)
proposals.motivation: Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) is a devastating, rapidly spreading disease threatening Caribbean reefs, yet its precise microbial etiology remains unclear. Prior small-scale metagenomic studies have identified candidate pathogens, but their findings require validation in a larger, geographically diverse cohort to distinguish causative agents from opportunistic colonizers. Deeper sequencing is critical to detect low-abundance but potentially pathogenic microbes that may be missed by standard depth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a larger cohort study with ultra-deep metagenomic sequencing will robustly identify and validate specific, low-abundance bacterial or viral taxa whose presence and abundance are consistently associated with SCTLD lesions across independent reefs.
proposals.method: We will perform deep whole-genome shotgun sequencing (≥100 Gb per sample) on a minimum of 200 coral tissue samples (healthy, diseased lesion, and apparently healthy tissue from diseased colonies) collected across multiple Caribbean sites. Sequence data will be analyzed using a comprehensive, multi-tiered bioinformatics pipeline: host reads will be filtered using a *Pseudodiploria strigosa* or *Orbicella faveolata* reference genome, followed by microbial profiling with Kraken2/Bracken against comprehensive genomic databases (RefSeq, GTDB). Association analysis will use tools like LEfSe and MaAsLin2 to correlate microbial features with disease state, controlling for site and colony effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the most statistically powerful assessment to date of the SCTLD microbiome, delivering a validated shortlist of microbial candidates for targeted culturing and experimental infection trials. It will establish a high-depth genomic resource and an analytical framework for investigating low-abundance pathogens in complex environmental samples, directly applicable to other coral diseases and wildlife epizootics.
proposals.novelty: While previous SCTLD studies have used metagenomics, this proposal combines an unprecedented sample size with sequencing depth an order of magnitude greater than typical environmental surveys, specifically powered to detect rare microbial members. This approach mirrors the cohort-scale, deep-sequencing strategies used in human microbiome-disease association studies but applies them to a critical environmental challenge.

proposals.title: Pan-Ecotype Genome Assembly and Annotation Reveals Extensive Structural Variation Undercaptured by a Single Reference
proposals.motivation: The reliance on a single reference genome for a species, often derived from one lab strain or ecotype, is a known but under-quantified bias in genomics. This is analogous to the limitations of the GRCh37/38 human references, which the Telomere-to-Telomere (T2T) consortium has begun to address by revealing missing sequences and structural variants. For non-model organisms, especially those with high ecological or biomedical relevance like corals (linked to SCTLD) or disease vectors, this bias can obscure critical genetic diversity underlying adaptation, disease susceptibility, and species definition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sequencing and assembling multiple, distinct ecotypes of a target species will reveal extensive, ecologically relevant structural and sequence variation that is absent or misrepresented in the current single reference genome, challenging its sufficiency for population-level genomic analyses.
proposals.method: We will select 3-5 divergent ecotypes of a well-studied species with an existing reference (e.g., a coral species or the *Aedes aegypti* mosquito, relevant to ZIKV). For each, we will generate high-coverage, long-read PacBio HiFi or Oxford Nanopore sequencing data, supplemented with Hi-C chromatin interaction data for scaffolding. We will perform *de novo* assembly for each ecotype using the Verkko or Hifiasm assemblers, followed by annotation transfer and refinement using the BRAKER2 pipeline. A multi-genome alignment tool like Cactus will be used to construct a pangenome graph, quantifying core and accessory sequences and cataloging large-scale structural variants (SVs) relative to the standard reference.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a high-quality, ecotype-aware pangenome resource, providing a more complete genomic landscape for the target species. It will directly quantify the fraction of genomic content and major SVs missed by the standard reference, offering a benchmark for the field. The results will inform the selection of appropriate reference genomes for functional studies (e.g., transcriptomics of healthy vs. diseased states) and population genetics.
proposals.novelty: While pangenome concepts are established in microbes and plants, their rigorous application to challenge the reference genome assumption for specific ecologically or medically important non-model eukaryotes, using contemporary complete assembly methods, remains less common. This project systematically tests the 'Columbia ecotype' problem by building multiple complete genomes as alternative references, moving beyond variant calling against a single linear coordinate system.

proposals.title: Uncovering the Hidden Proteome: A Systematic Search for Non-Canonical Genes in Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) and Zika Virus (ZIKV) Genomes
proposals.motivation: Current gene annotation pipelines, which rely heavily on reference genomes and canonical signals like start/stop codons, are demonstrably incomplete. This is a critical limitation for understanding complex biological phenomena, such as the pathogenesis of SCTLD in corals or the immune evasion strategies of viruses like ZIKV, where novel, non-canonical open reading frames (ORFs) could encode key virulence factors or regulatory elements. A comprehensive annotation is foundational for functional analysis and hypothesis generation.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic, ab initio searches of genomic and transcriptomic data from SCTLD-affected corals and ZIKV isolates will reveal a significant number of previously unannotated, functional non-canonical genes, including those overlapping known genes, using alternative start codons, or residing in non-coding RNAs.
proposals.method: We will apply a multi-pronged computational pipeline to existing and newly generated whole-genome and RNA-seq datasets. First, we will use de novo transcript assemblers (StringTie2) on coral RNA-seq data to identify novel transcripts. Second, we will employ ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq) data analysis tools (RiboTaper) on available ZIKV-infected cell datasets to detect translated ORFs. Third, we will implement deep learning models (inspired by DeepRibo or ORFfinder enhancements) to predict translation potential from sequence and conservation features across multiple strains, integrating evidence from protein-level mass spectrometry data where available.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a significantly expanded and more accurate annotation for key SCTLD and ZIKV genomic resources, directly enabling downstream studies on disease mechanisms. It will also deliver and benchmark a flexible computational framework for non-canonical gene discovery that can be applied to other genomes, challenging the assumption of annotation completeness in genomics.
proposals.novelty: While searches for novel genes are common, this proposal uniquely focuses on applying the latest pre-2026 integrative computational methods to the specific, ecologically and medically urgent contexts of coral disease and arbovirus genomics, where the potential for overlooked genetic elements is high and their impact profound.

proposals.title: Functional Annotation of the 'Dark Proteome' in Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) through Integrated Transcriptomics and Protein Structure Prediction
proposals.motivation: Despite extensive sequencing efforts, a large fraction of genes in any newly sequenced genome, including those of corals and their pathogens, lack functional annotation, limiting our understanding of key biological processes. In the context of the devastating Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD), this knowledge gap is critical, as uncharacterized genes may encode virulence factors, host defense mechanisms, or biomarkers of health. Pre-2026 studies by our group and others have generated comprehensive transcriptomes from SCTLD-affected and healthy coral samples, revealing a high proportion of genes of unknown function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the uncharacterized transcriptome in the SCTLD holobiont encodes proteins with structural motifs and expression patterns indicative of specific roles in disease progression, host response, or symbiosis disruption.
proposals.method: We will apply a multi-tiered computational pipeline to our existing SCTLD RNA-seq datasets. First, we will use deep learning-based protein structure prediction (AlphaFold2) on the translated sequences of unannotated genes to identify structural homologs and potential functional domains. Second, we will integrate these structural predictions with differential expression analysis (using tools like DESeq2) between diseased and healthy states to prioritize candidate genes. Third, we will perform co-expression network analysis (WGCNA) to link unknown genes to modules of genes with known functions, inferring putative biological roles.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first functionally informed catalog of the 'dark proteome' associated with SCTLD, moving beyond simple sequence homology. It will provide testable hypotheses for experimental validation, identifying specific uncharacterized genes as high-priority targets for understanding disease mechanisms. The integrated computational framework will serve as a model for functional discovery in other non-model organisms and disease systems.
proposals.novelty: While AlphaFold2 has been used for single proteins, its systematic application to annotate thousands of unknown transcripts from an environmental disease system, combined with network-based expression analysis, is a novel and powerful approach. This moves functional prediction from sequence similarity to structure-informed biological context, a significant advance over standard BLAST-based annotation pipelines.

proposals.title: Characterizing the Origin, Timing, and Functional Impact of a Large Mitochondrial DNA Insertion in a Coral Genome
proposals.motivation: Large-scale nuclear insertions of mitochondrial DNA (NUMTs) are recognized as drivers of genomic variation and potential sources of genetic disease, but their origins and functional consequences in non-model organisms remain poorly understood. The recent discovery of a substantial mitochondrial genome insertion in a coral species, a key organism for reef health and symbiosis studies, provides a unique opportunity to investigate the mechanisms and impacts of such events in an ecologically vital and genomically complex system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the large mitochondrial insertion is a recent, lineage-specific event that has altered the local nuclear genomic architecture and may have functional consequences for the coral's cellular metabolism or stress response pathways.
proposals.method: We will assemble a high-quality, chromosome-level reference genome for the target coral using PacBio HiFi long-read sequencing and Hi-C scaffolding. We will map the insertion's precise boundaries and flanking sequences using BLAST and custom alignment scripts. To date the insertion, we will perform phylogenetic analysis of the inserted mitochondrial sequence against the native mitochondrial genome and orthologous nuclear loci from related coral species, estimating divergence times using BEAST2. Functional impact will be assessed by analyzing RNA-seq data from healthy and stressed (e.g., SCTLD-exposed) coral samples to quantify expression of genes near the insertion and detect potential fusion transcripts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed characterization of a major NUMT event in a coral, establishing a framework for studying genomic plasticity in marine invertebrates. It will contribute a new high-quality reference genome to the community and yield insights into how such insertions arise and whether they play a role in the genomic response to environmental stressors like disease.
proposals.novelty: While NUMTs are known, systematically tracing the origin and functional impact of a specific, large insertion in a non-model, ecologically critical organism like coral, and linking it to environmental phenotype data, represents a novel integration of comparative genomics and functional analysis.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Footprinting of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) Resistance Loci in Caribbean Corals
proposals.motivation: Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) is a devastating, pan-Caribbean epidemic threatening reef ecosystems. While some coral species and individuals show differential susceptibility, the genetic basis of resistance, particularly in non-coding regulatory regions, remains poorly characterized. Identifying these functional elements is critical for understanding disease dynamics and informing conservation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cross-species sequence conservation analysis (phylogenetic footprinting) across susceptible and resistant Caribbean coral species will reveal conserved non-coding elements (CNEs) linked to immune and stress response genes, and that these CNEs are enriched for genetic variation associated with SCTLD outcome.
proposals.method: We will generate and assemble high-quality, chromosome-scale genome sequences for key Caribbean coral species with varying SCTLD susceptibility (e.g., *Orbicella faveolata*, *Pseudodiploria strigosa*) using PacBio HiFi and Hi-C sequencing. A multiple genome alignment of these and existing coral genomes will be constructed using progressive Cactus. Phylogenetic footprinting will be performed using phyloP to identify CNEs. These CNEs will be overlapped with whole-genome sequencing data from healthy and SCTLD-affected coral colonies to test for genotype-phenotype associations, and their proximity to genes will inform functional annotation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new genomic resource of high-quality coral genomes and deliver the first map of evolutionarily conserved regulatory elements associated with disease resistance in corals. It will identify candidate non-coding loci for experimental validation and provide a framework for applying comparative genomics to non-model marine organisms facing novel pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic footprinting is established in human genetics, its application to decipher disease resistance in ecologically critical, non-model marine invertebrates is novel. This approach directly leverages Dr. Salzberg's expertise in genome assembly, comparative analysis, and phenotype-genotype mapping to address a pressing environmental challenge.

proposals.title: De novo gene prediction in coral-associated prokaryotic endosymbionts: A computational pipeline for annotating compact, non-model genomes
proposals.motivation: The health of coral reef ecosystems is critically linked to their associated prokaryotic microbiomes, yet many of these microbial genomes remain poorly annotated due to a lack of high-quality reference sequences. This gap hinders our understanding of functional roles in processes like nutrient cycling and disease response, such as in Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD). Ab initio gene prediction, proven effective for compact viral and bacterial genomes, offers a path to functional annotation where homology-based methods fail.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ab initio gene prediction algorithms, trained on known prokaryotic genomes and optimized for compact, AT/GC-biased sequences, can accurately identify protein-coding genes in newly sequenced, non-model prokaryotic endosymbionts of corals, providing a foundation for functional genomic analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline leveraging established ab initio algorithms like Glimmer and GeneMark, which use interpolated Markov models for gene boundary detection. The pipeline will be trained on curated sets of compact bacterial and archaeal genomes, then applied to whole-genome sequencing data from prokaryotes associated with healthy and SCTLD-affected corals. Annotation will be validated against available transcriptomic data and protein family databases (e.g., Pfam) to refine predictions and assess accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, open-source pipeline for the annotation of non-model prokaryotic coral symbionts, generating the first comprehensive gene catalogs for key microbial taxa. These annotations will enable downstream comparative and functional analyses to identify putative virulence factors, metabolic pathways, and biomarkers of coral health, directly supporting disease ecology research.
proposals.novelty: While ab initio prediction is established for model prokaryotes, its systematic application and optimization for the diverse, often extreme, genomic landscapes of coral-associated prokaryotes—integrating with host-focused 'phenotype to genotype' approaches—is novel and addresses a critical bottleneck in marine microbiome research.

proposals.title: HaploBin: Resolving Diploid and Polyploid Haplotypes in Complex Samples Using Metagenomic Binning Strategies
proposals.motivation: Accurate haplotype phasing is critical for understanding genetic diversity, disease association, and evolution in diploid and polyploid organisms, including humans, corals, and pathogens like Zika virus (ZIKV). Current methods struggle with complex, heterogeneous samples—such as coral colonies affected by Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) or mixed viral populations—where reads from multiple distinct haplotypes are intermingled. This limits our ability to link specific genetic variants to phenotypes or to reconstruct complete, haplotype-resolved genomes from environmental or clinical metagenomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that algorithms developed for binning sequences from distinct microbial species in metagenomic assemblies, which leverage sequence composition (k-mer spectra) and co-abundance patterns across samples, can be effectively repurposed to separate sequencing reads or contigs originating from different haplotypes within a single eukaryotic or viral species.
proposals.method: We will adapt and benchmark established metagenomic binning tools (e.g., MetaBAT2, MaxBin2, CONCOCT) that use sequence composition (tetranucleotide frequency) and differential coverage across multiple related samples. The pipeline will be tested on: 1) simulated diploid/polyploid datasets, 2) real human diploid genome sequencing data (leveraging the CHM13 complete human genome as a reference benchmark), and 3) complex coral holobiont samples (healthy vs. SCTLD-affected) sequenced in our prior work. We will integrate binning outputs with variant calls and linkage information to reconstruct phased haplotype blocks, comparing performance against existing dedicated phasing tools like HapCUT2 and WhatsHap.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available computational pipeline for haplotype resolution in complex samples, directly applicable to coral genomics, viral quasispecies analysis (e.g., ZIKV, HIV-1), and human genetic studies. It will enable more accurate genotype-to-phenotype mapping in non-model, polyploid, or mixed-population contexts, advancing personalized genomics and disease ecology.
proposals.novelty: While metagenomic binning is standard for separating species, its application to separate haplotypes *within* a species is a novel conceptual crossover. This approach is non-obvious because it treats genetic variation within a single organism or viral population as analogous to variation between microbial taxa, leveraging co-abundance signals from multi-sample sequencing cohorts.

proposals.title: A Benchmark for Minimal Microbial Genomes: Creating Perfect References for Small, Repetitive Pathogens
proposals.motivation: High-quality reference genomes are critical for genomic analysis, yet for many small, repetitive microbial pathogens, such as viruses and bacteria, existing references are fragmented or contain errors. This is a major obstacle for accurate variant calling, transcriptome annotation, and phenotype-genotype association studies, as demonstrated in pre-2026 work on HIV-1 and Zika virus (ZIKV). A standardized benchmark, akin to the recently developed diploid human genome benchmark, is urgently needed for these simpler but technically challenging genomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying a rigorous, multi-platform sequencing and assembly validation strategy to carefully selected microbial samples, we can generate a collection of 'perfect' reference genomes that will serve as an authoritative benchmark for evaluating sequencing methods and variant calls in microbial genomics.
proposals.method: We will select diverse, high-quality samples of small, repetitive microbial genomes, including viruses (e.g., specific HIV-1 clades, ZIKV) and bacteria with challenging repeats. For each, we will generate deep, multi-platform sequencing data (e.g., PacBio HiFi, Oxford Nanopore, Illumina). Assemblies will be constructed using a suite of tools (e.g., Canu, Flye, metaFlye) and reconciled into a single, manually curated consensus sequence. This consensus will be validated using orthogonal data, including optical maps and, where applicable, matched RNA-seq data for transcriptome confirmation, following the philosophy established in the Salzberg lab's recent benchmark projects.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first community resource of benchmark-quality microbial genomes, enabling objective evaluation of sequencing platforms, assemblers, and variant callers for pathogens. It will directly improve the accuracy of genomic studies for critical infectious diseases and provide a model for expanding benchmarks to other non-model organisms, such as coral symbionts relevant to stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD).
proposals.novelty: While benchmarks exist for complex eukaryotic genomes, this systematically applies the benchmark philosophy—combining multi-platform sequencing, manual curation, and orthogonal validation—to the distinct challenges of small, repetitive microbial genomes, creating a new standard for the field.

proposals.title: A High-Resolution Benchmark for Clinically Relevant Genomic Loci: Validation of Targeted Sequencing and Variant Calling
proposals.motivation: While whole-genome benchmarks like GRCh38 provide a foundation, clinical and conservation genomics increasingly rely on targeted sequencing of specific loci, such as disease-associated genes or hypervariable viral regions. Existing benchmarks lack the depth and focused truth sets needed to rigorously assess accuracy in these critical, often complex, regions. This gap hinders the development and validation of precision diagnostics and surveillance tools for pathogens like Zika virus (ZIKV) or conditions like stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by creating ultra-deep, multi-platform truth sets for targeted genomic regions, we can establish a benchmark that reveals systematic errors in variant calling and assembly that are obscured in whole-genome assessments, leading to significant improvements in accuracy for clinical and environmental applications.
proposals.method: We will select a panel of high-impact loci, including human clinically relevant genes (e.g., BRCA1, TP53), conserved coral health markers, and complete genomes of pathogens like ZIKV. For each, we will generate a truth set by integrating deep coverage from multiple long-read (PacBio HiFi, Oxford Nanopore) and short-read (Illumina) platforms, leveraging the ensemble and reconciliation methods pioneered in the CHM13 human genome project. Variant calling and assembly performance will be evaluated using established pipelines (e.g., GATK, minimap2) against this curated truth, with a focus on challenging variants like short indels in homopolymers and structural variants in repeat regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available, high-accuracy benchmark specifically for targeted genomic analysis, enabling rigorous optimization of sequencing assays for precision medicine and environmental monitoring. It will directly benefit the development of reliable diagnostics for genetic diseases and pathogen surveillance, and provide a model for creating focused benchmarks for any organism.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing whole-genome benchmarks, this proposal shifts the paradigm to locus-specific validation, which is the actual use case in most applied genomics. It combines the consortium-based truth-setting approach from human genomics with the targeted, phenotype-driven sequencing strategies used in viral and coral health research.

ideas.title: Assemble the Transcriptome Using a 'Patchwork' Reference from Related Species
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from historical linguistics, where fragmented ancient texts are reconstructed using quotations found in later, diverse sources. The analogous problem of building a comprehensive transcriptome without a high-quality genome assembly is solved by creating a composite 'patchwork' reference from the annotated genomes and transcriptomes of multiple phylogenetically close species. Computational tools would map sequencing reads to this composite reference, leveraging conserved synteny and sequence homology to identify exons and splice variants. Through an iterative process of conflict resolution and gap-filling guided by the RNA-seq data itself, a coherent transcriptome for the target species can be assembled, enabling gene annotation independently of its challenging, fragmented genome.

ideas.title: Gene Annotation via Fragment Assembly and Contextual Inference
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that accurate gene annotation fundamentally requires long genomic scaffolds. Drawing an analogy from archaeology, where the form and function of complete artifacts are reconstructed from small fragments using contextual clues and statistical knowledge of known types, we propose a computational solution for genomics. The adapted research idea is to develop a probabilistic, machine learning framework that bypasses the scaffold assembly step. This framework would be trained on comprehensive gene annotations from model organisms and would directly analyze short-read sequences—looking at k-mer patterns, read pair information, and integrated data like chromatin accessibility or RNA-seq—to statistically infer and annotate gene models, including complex features like alternative splicing, even in the absence of a contiguous reference.

ideas.title: Assemble the genome of a pine fungal pathogen using a hybrid, multi-platform approach
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a hybrid reconstruction methodology from archaeology to solve the genomic scaling challenge of assembling a pine fungal pathogen's genome. In archaeology, complete artifacts are reconstructed by fusing high-resolution surface imaging with long-range structural scans. Analogously, we propose to sequence the pathogen's genome using a multi-platform hybrid approach: combining high-accuracy short-reads (Illumina) for detailed base-pair resolution with long-reads (PacBio/Oxford Nanopore) and optical mapping (Bionano) to resolve repetitive regions and provide long-range structural context. Specialized bioinformatics pipelines will be developed to fuse these complementary data types, overcoming the limitations of any single technology to produce a complete, chromosomal-scale assembly. This high-quality genome will serve as the foundational resource for large-scale, systems-level studies of host-pathogen interactions.

ideas.title: Long-read metagenomic profiling of host-microbe-repetitive element interactions in a large disease cohort
ideas.description: This study will apply deep, long-read sequencing (e.g., PacBio HiFi, Oxford Nanopore) to a large, well-phenotyped patient cohort to achieve two integrated objectives. It will sensitively profile the entire microbiome, including low-abundance and hard-to-sequence taxa, while concurrently generating phased, high-quality assemblies of the highly repetitive (82%) host genome for each individual. This dual approach allows us to test the novel hypothesis that microbial presence or abundance is linked to the activity, expression, or structural variation of specific host repetitive elements (e.g., endogenous retroviruses, satellite DNA) in disease states. The synergy creates a unified framework to explore the co-evolution and potential interaction between the metagenome and the repetitive 'dark matter' of the host genome at population scale.

ideas.title: A Haploid Reference Genome to Decode Host-Microbiome Dysbiosis in a Model Conifer
ideas.description: This project proposes to sequence and assemble a high-quality, haploid reference genome for the smallest known conifer species. Using this definitive genetic blueprint as a foundation, we will then characterize the native microbial community (microbiome) of healthy individuals and track its compositional and functional shifts under environmental stress or disease states. The synergy lies in using the complete host genome to filter host sequences with extreme precision from metagenomic data, allowing for an unambiguous, high-resolution view of dysbiosis. This model system tests the core hypothesis that disease is driven by imbalance in native communities, while providing a powerful genomic toolkit to identify the host genetic regulators of microbiome stability.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Family Functional Site Prediction from Structural Biology to NLR Annotation
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from structural biology's protein function prediction to the problem of annotating the large NLR gene family in pine. In structural biology, tools like ConSurf are used to analyze conserved structural motifs and active site residues in protein families (e.g., kinases) to infer the specific function of uncharacterized members. We adapt this by applying structural bioinformatics and motif-conservation analysis to pine NLRs. Using predicted or template-based 3D models, we can identify conserved surface patches and residue positions critical for pathogen recognition, such as in the leucine-rich repeat regions. This functional site prediction will then guide the annotation of pine NLRs into putative specificity classes based on their predicted binding interfaces, providing a more functionally informative annotation than standard sequence homology methods from crop genomics.

ideas.title: Probabilistic functional element annotation in fragmented assemblies using deep spectral learning
ideas.description: This approach reframes the problem from one of gene annotation to one of functional element detection, accepting the inherent ambiguity of short-read assemblies. A neural network is trained on features like k-mer frequency, co-occurrence patterns from paired-end reads, and cross-species conservation signals extracted directly from short-read alignments, without assuming contiguity. The system outputs a map of probable coding regions, regulatory motifs, and non-coding RNAs as localized probabilities across the fragmented scaffolds, explicitly labeling their uncertain boundaries and connections. This provides a practical, statistically grounded functional guide for a highly fragmented genome, where traditional annotation is impossible.

ideas.title: Genome-wide marker-assisted breeding for host-associated microbiomes in tree populations
ideas.description: This research direction applies the principles of genome-wide marker selection from industrial tree breeding to a new target: the tree's microbiome. It involves conducting a large-scale cohort study of genetically characterized tree populations, using advanced, deep-sequencing metagenomics to profile both abundant and rare microbial members. The goal is to identify host genetic markers (QTLs or SNPs) that are associated with the recruitment and maintenance of specific, beneficial microbial consortia, such as those enhancing nitrogen fixation, disease resistance, or drought tolerance. This enables a novel breeding paradigm where trees are selectively bred for optimized symbiotic partnerships, enhancing resilience and productivity through genetic manipulation of the host to shape its microbial environment.

Adapted to microbial genomics, the research would employ a bioinformatic 'interferometry' pipeline, combining multi-replicate sequencing data with sophisticated computational filtering and statistical signal-stacking models. This approach will enhance the effective sequencing depth and sensitivity specifically for low-abundance microbes, enabling more robust detection and validation in a large, heterogeneous cohort than standard deeper sequencing alone.

ideas.title: Integrated Survey and Landmark Mapping for Genome Assembly
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that shotgun sequencing must be the primary, isolated phase of genome assembly. Drawing an analogy from integrated cartography, where aerial imagery and ground control surveys are conducted together, we propose that whole-genome shotgun sequencing and long-range optical mapping should be combined from the very start as a unified primary strategy. The analogous solution from GIS involves simultaneous acquisition of dense local data (aerial imagery/shotgun reads) and accurate long-range spatial data (LIDAR control points/optical maps) for immediate integration. Adapted to genomics, this means preparing a single DNA sample for concurrent short-read sequencing and optical mapping, then using the long-range map not as a post-hoc fix but as a foundational scaffold during initial assembly. This integrated data pipeline would correct local assembly errors in real-time, resolve repeats more reliably by providing immediate long-range context, and produce a more accurate and contiguous assembly in a single streamlined process.

ideas.title: Characterize the full repetitive element landscape using signal processing techniques
ideas.description: Expand the analysis of the highly repetitive genome (82%) by drawing an analogy from digital signal processing. In audio engineering, Fourier and wavelet transforms decompose complex sounds into fundamental frequencies and patterns to separate overlapping signals. Adapting this solution, treat the genomic sequence as a one-dimensional signal. Apply spectral and wavelet analysis to computationally decompose the DNA sequence, enabling the precise identification, classification, and boundary definition of all repetitive element families—including ancient, fragmented, and nested repeats—without sole reliance on reference databases. This approach will characterize the full, complex landscape of repetitive DNA, revealing patterns of expansion, contraction, and evolutionary change that are opaque to current alignment-based methods.

ideas.title: Deconvolution of Diploid Genomes via Computational Phasing, Inspired by Astronomical Signal Separation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that haploid megagametophyte tissue is essential for assembling complex diploid pine genomes by drawing an analogy from astronomy. In radio astronomy, signals from multiple sources are received as a single mixed interferometric signal and are separated using deconvolution algorithms like CLEAN to reconstruct individual images. Adapting this solution, we treat the diploid genome as a 'mixed signal' of two parental haplotypes. The research idea is to develop and apply advanced computational phasing and assembly algorithms—inspired by astronomical signal separation techniques—that can statistically deconvolve, separate, and independently assemble the two haplotypes directly from sequencing data derived from complex diploid pine tissue, thereby bypassing the dependency on haploid biological material.

ideas.title: Adapting Astronomical Spectral Unmixing to Classify Pine Genome Repeats
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astronomy and remote sensing—spectral unmixing for hyperspectral imagery—to the problem of classifying repetitive elements in the large and complex pine genome. In the analogous field, algorithms decompose the mixed light spectrum from a pixel into the pure spectra of constituent materials and their abundances. Here, we adapt this approach by treating genomic sequence fragments as 'pixels,' their k-mer composition as a 'spectral signature,' and sequencing coverage from multiple libraries as 'abundance data.' The adapted method would apply linear or non-negative matrix factorization unmixing techniques to identify the fundamental repeat family signatures (endmembers) and map their proportional representation across the genome, effectively binning and quantifying intertwined repeats in a manner analogous to resolving mixed pixels in a planetary image.

ideas.title: Disentangling Episodic Re-infection from Persistent Colonization Using Controlled Microbial Clearance and Multi-omics Trajectories
ideas.description: This research program is designed to test the 'episodic infection' hypothesis against the 'occult persistence' alternative by establishing a causal experimental framework. It uses gnotobiotic animals colonized with a traceable microbial community, subjected to scheduled, effective antibiotic pulses to eliminate the target pathogen. Subsequent re-introduction or natural reacquisition from cage mates models episodic infection. The core innovation is the integration of ultra-sensitive pathogen detection (ddPCR, targeted metagenomics) with deep host response profiling (serology, tissue-resident memory cell assays, inflammatory mediators) to create a trajectory map for each phase. This allows the damage signature of a true re-infection (acute response to a previously absent pathogen) to be distinguished from the flare of a suppressed but persistent infection.

ideas.title: Adapting Astronomical Spectroscopy to Sequence Giant Pine Chromosomes
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astronomy/astrophysics, where integral field spectroscopy is used to analyze extended celestial objects by breaking their light into spatially resolved spectra. The analogous problem in genomics is the overwhelming size and complexity of individual giant pine chromosomes, which are difficult to sequence as intact units. The adapted research idea is to develop a method that takes an isolated giant chromosome, partitions it linearly into hundreds of contiguous segments within a microfluidic device, and performs parallel, barcoded sequencing on all segments in a single run. This 'genomic integral field sequencing' approach would allow the reconstruction of the chromosome's sequence and structure by treating it as an extended linear object to be mapped segment by segment, overcoming the scaling challenge posed by its enormous size.

ideas.title: Environment-Aware Genomic Selection for Robust Industrial Tree Breeding
ideas.description: This research develops a genomic selection system that explicitly models genotype-by-environment (GxE) interactions to ensure robust performance predictions across diverse industrial planting landscapes. It integrates high-resolution environmental data from a network of field trials with genome-wide marker information to build predictive models that are conditional on specific deployment environments. The approach enables breeders to select parent trees optimized not just for average performance, but for predictable success in targeted soil and climate conditions. This results in more reliable genetic gains and reduced risk of genotype failure when scaled to operational plantations.

ideas.title: Functional validation of conifer-specific gene families via a synthetic biology chassis approach
ideas.description: The identified conifer-unique gene families require experimental validation to determine their biological functions. Drawing an analogy from microbial ecology, where genes from unculturable organisms are expressed in a tractable chassis like E. coli for functional study, this idea proposes a parallel solution. By adapting this synthetic biology chassis approach, conifer-specific genes can be cloned and heterologously expressed in a fast-growing, genetically tractable model plant system (e.g., Arabidopsis or Nicotiana). This bypasses the major experimental bottlenecks of conifer biology, enabling high-throughput phenotypic screening to link these unique genes to specific traits such as drought resistance, pathogen defense, or the biosynthesis of specialized compounds like terpenes.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic 'Silent Earthquake' Detection to Map Episodic Subclinical Infections
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that progressive damage from microbes requires chronic persistence by proposing damage is caused by recurrent, subclinical acute infections. It draws an analogy from geophysics, where the discovery of 'silent earthquakes' (slow slip events) explained tectonic strain release not detected by traditional seismographs. The analogous solution involved dense, sensitive sensor networks and novel signal processing. Adapted to infection biology, the research idea is to deploy a 'clinical sensor network' for high-frequency longitudinal multi-omic profiling in patient cohorts. By applying seismic-style signal detection algorithms to this biological timeseries data, the goal is to map the precise episodic signatures of subclinical infections and link these events directly to markers of progressive tissue or immune damage, validating the episodic model.

ideas.title: Tear Film Pathogen Detection via Surface Plasmon Resonance Biosensing
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a pathogen must reside within the corneal epithelium by hypothesizing its presence in the tear film. Drawing an analogy from environmental microbiology, where SPR biosensors detect sparse, free-floating pathogens in water, we adapt this solution. By using a tear-fluid-compatible SPR biosensor functionalized with pathogen-specific antibodies, we can directly probe for the pathogen's presence in the tear film, providing a method to validate or refute the hypothesis without relying on corneal cell culture or biopsy.
